new_findings.title: Four-axis sub qualification (profession, location, age, hobbies) to size wallet and build rapport

variants.claim: Systematically qualifying subscribers on four data points — profession, location, age, and hobbies — lets chatters estimate spending capacity and tailor their communication style before attempting to sell.
variants.what_they_do: After the personalized opener gets a response, chatters move into a structured qualification sequence. They ask about: (1) Profession — men spend 40+ hrs/week on work and will talk about it freely; also signals income level. (2) Location — dictates tone, cultural references, and timezone. (3) Age — a 52-year-old has 30 more years of earning history than a 22-year-old; older subs get more formal language, younger subs get slang. (4) Hobbies — reveals personality and gives commonality-building material. Chatters are also taught 'level two qualification' — drilling one level deeper on each topic (e.g., 'How did you get into that job?') to extract more information and signal genuine interest.

variants.claim: Selling is most effective when chatters first collect structured fan data (name, location, age, job, kinks) to gauge spending power and build connection, instead of spamming PPVs on entry.
variants.what_they_do: Uses systemized new-subscriber scripts with pre-written replies so every chatter sounds identical to the model, collects data to estimate purchasing power (e.g. country/job), records it in per-fan notes, and only escalates to a PPV ladder that builds up to ~$200.

variants.claim: Talking to a fan before quoting any price lets chatters learn his job/lifestyle and set a higher first PPV price ($25 vs $10) because no anchor was established early.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters ask open questions about job and hobbies — not from a script, but organically — to extract spending-power signals. Light flirting and jokes are used; sexual escalation is avoided until the fan initiates. The chatter never names a price before this rapport window closes.

sources.quote: number one profession you will be talking to men okay simps Subs they are men
another one is age how old are they part of the reason of qualifying is that we want to see their potential as a customer
what's going to make you money is data, right? Data collection
you can get the scripts all the way up to $200
we do use scripts inside our agency, but we never have the rapport part scripted as it's really important to let the conversation run smoothly
you're also going to get a glimpse of his spending power. So for how much you can send him PPVs

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal baseline at Palm for chatter qualification frameworks (§8, outsourced). Build-toward for in-house chat team.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a qualification checklist into the in-house chatter training SOP: chatters must capture profession, location, age, and at least one hobby for every sub before escalating to selling. Store this data per-fan in the Fan Tracker CRM (§9) to enable personalized re-engagement. Age and profession data should also feed the whale-hunting logic — a 50-year-old professional is a higher-priority cultivation target than an 18-year-old student.

new_findings.title: Golden Ratio: 60 DMs/day for 30 days guarantees first client

variants.claim: Sending 60 outreach DMs per day (10/hour, 6 hours/day, 7 days/week) produces 1,680 messages per month, which is sufficient to sign at least one client.
variants.what_they_do: Agency instructs new operators to hit exactly 60 DMs per day split into 6 one-hour blocks. At that pace, 1,680 monthly contacts makes signing a single client near-certain. They also recommend tracking every outreach in a spreadsheet and following up with non-responders.

variants.claim: Sending ~50 DMs per day from a personal IG account is the optimal beginner outreach volume, and you should drop 5 below whatever number triggers Instagram's rate-limit warning to avoid restrictions.
variants.what_they_do: Operator DMs from their personal IG account targeting creators with 5k–50k followers who mention or have OnlyFans. They monitor for Instagram's 'Check community guidelines / actions required' warning; whatever number triggered it, they permanently send 5 fewer. Follow-up sequence: wait 24 hours after no reply, then send a voice message for personalization.

variants.claim: Cold outreach signs creators predictably only when run as a system of high volume, long consistency, and value-first conversation rather than clever one-off scripts.
variants.what_they_do: Commits to 100 personalized DMs/day for 100 days (25/account across 4 accounts), value-first openers via voice/video note, and finding the avatar through conversation (10-30% qualify) rather than pre-filtering on the profile; built a 500k/mo agency on cold outreach before running a single ad.

sources.quote: For 30 days, if you do six 60 outreach messages a day, you can do 10 per hour, six times a day
I want you to send 50 DMs per day
always send five below. Whenever you get that message
wait 24 hours and then send another follow-up from that point on. a voice message
the real unit of measurement is 100 personalized DMs a day for 100 days
voice and video cut through 10 times better than text

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is Josh-DM-led with no codified daily volume target or tracking cadence (§6). No 'golden ratio' equivalent exists internally — outreach volume is ad hoc.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Codify a minimum daily outreach quota (e.g., 60 DMs/day) for whoever owns acquisition at Palm. Build or adopt a simple lead tracker (Airtable view or spreadsheet) to log status and follow-ups, matching the systematic volume this competitor prescribes.

new_findings.topic: Fan segmentation — low vs high spenders
new_findings.title: Segment fans by max spend ($50 vs $120+) and use different opening tactics per tier

variants.claim: Low/non-spenders need fast, transactional, no-brainer openings, while high spenders need slower, personalized, emotionally invested approaches that reflect the higher ROI of putting time into them.
variants.what_they_do: Two distinct opening playbooks: (1) Low/non-spenders (<$50 max purchase): 'Two choices' illusion (free thing + heavily discounted PPV framed as a favor); 'Question game' (ask 2–3 preference questions about color/toy/angle, disappear briefly, return with a PPV 'chosen based on their answers'). Goal is just to get the first purchase and enter the loop. (2) High spenders (>$120 max purchase): 'Opinion opening' (frame a PPV as something the creator is nervous/unsure about, ask for their trusted opinion — if they object to price, say you're 'impartial' and it makes them more curious); 'Personalized PPV' for lapsed high spenders (manually send a high-quality video with a caption referencing their past purchases, their interests from fan notes, priced $150–200, with fan's name in the first 5 words of the last message sent).

variants.claim: Organizing the subscriber inbox into five explicit priority tiers ensures chatters always work highest-revenue conversations first and never waste time on non-spenders while whales wait.
variants.what_they_do: Priority order: (1) fans currently in a sext/purchase session, (2) fans being transitioned into a session (teasing phase), (3) new subscribers (unknown spend potential — test them), (4) known spenders/whales in relationship-building, (5) time-wasters — only addressed when inbox is empty. Agency adjusts exact ordering based on where their revenue concentrates.

variants.claim: When managing multiple accounts simultaneously, chatters should prioritize responses to high-spending or long-tenure subscribers over the general queue.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters explicitly rank their open conversations: whales and loyal long-term members get first responses. Low-spend or new subscribers are handled after, often with templated messages to maintain efficiency without abandoning them.

sources.quote: if we were talking to, say, a low or non-spender, we want to be able to transition in a way that is fast and efficient and make that first pay-per-view seem like a no-brainer
it's pretty much exclusively for non-active high spenders. So people that used to spend a lot of money, but don't really talk anymore
number one in my opinion for everybody should be the ones that are currently in a sexting session
The last ones should be time wasters because they're just wasting our time
prioritize high value chats focus on subscribers who frequently spend money or longtime members

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a Fan Tracker CRM and whale-hunting module (§9) that tracks spend data, but no documented segmented selling playbook or per-tier opener scripts. The data infrastructure exists; the chatter execution layer does not.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Map these two tiers onto Palm's existing Fan Tracker/whale-hunting data. For the in-house chat team, write two distinct opener script banks keyed to max-spend threshold. The 'question game' opener is particularly low-friction and could be A/B tested quickly once chatting is brought in-house. The lapsed-whale personalized PPV ($150–200, notes-referenced caption) is a high-ROI re-engagement tactic worth standardizing.

new_findings.topic: Creator brand building as a distinct discipline from niche selection
new_findings.title: Build distinct creator brands (audience + identity + story) — not just niches

variants.claim: Most OFM agencies confuse 'brand' with 'niche,' but a real creator brand answers three questions: who is your audience, what is the creator's unique identity, and what story/fantasy are you selling.
variants.what_they_do: The consultant uses a three-question brand framework — (1) specific audience demographic beyond 'men who like porn', (2) unique creator identity that inspires devotion, (3) specific fantasy/story and emotional levers to pull — and claims this framework has taken creators from zero to top 0.1% earning hundreds of thousands per month. He cites Camila and Bonnie Blue as examples of brand-driven success.

variants.claim: Posting high volume of content (10 TikToks/day, 10 IG Reels/day) plateaus revenue at ~$10K/mo; defining who the creator actually is — her vibe, niche, audience, and what makes her different — is what breaks past that ceiling.
variants.what_they_do: Griffin forced Barry to stop posting entirely and answer 'who is this model?' before resuming. They built a persona/brand sheet for each creator covering identity, vibe, niche, and differentiator. Content strategy only resumes once that identity is locked. They report LTV increases of 20–30% and improved CVR after implementing this.

variants.claim: A distinct creator persona (identity, consistent theme, story) is replacing raw posting volume as the driver of growth, conversion, and ban-resistance.
variants.what_they_do: Defines a brand as persona + consistent theme + story (e.g. dominant/funny/confident); reports a branded creator going 2k to 50k/mo in two months, and writes a one-sentence persona applied to bios, captions, content, and chats.

variants.claim: Building a written character/persona for each creator and aligning all content to it raises LTV by enabling a parasocial relationship rather than generic thirst traps.
variants.what_they_do: Hive Media sits each new model down to create an in-depth character sheet (occupation, personality, persona, backstory) and aligns outfits, scenes, and PPVs to it, deliberately targeting spending demographics by the model's look so chatters can monetize a fleshed-out character.

sources.quote: most of OFM doesn't know what brand actually means or confuses the concept of brand with niche
brand and only fans boils down to three questions. The first question is who is your audience? The second question is what is your identity? The third and final question is what is your story?
Stop posting for a second and tell me who the model actually is
figuring out her identity such as her vibe her audience her niche and then yeah just what makes her different
make it that her persona
you're actually a character with a story and depth... build a better parasocial relationship with them

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented creator brand framework (§2 GAP and §1 partial). The content pipeline (§1) is strong on viral ideation but does not appear to systematically define creator identity, target audience persona, or narrative/fantasy positioning before deciding what content to film. This is a meaningful strategic gap in how Palm directs creators.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt a structured brand-definition step upstream of the content pipeline (§1). Before a creator enters the inspo/filming cycle, document: (1) their specific target audience segment, (2) their unique identity differentiators vs. comparable creators, (3) the core fantasy/emotional narrative they sell. Store this in the Creators table in Airtable and use it as a filter when promoting inspo reels to creators in Step 5 of the pipeline.

new_findings.title: An AI chatbot can replace much of the chat team at a fraction of the cost

variants.claim: A well-configured AI chatting bot can handle 24/7 fan conversations and PPV upsells at a fraction of a chat team's cost, but only if it is carefully set up and trained.
variants.what_they_do: Uses Super Creator's 'Izzy' AI chatbot (~$200-300/month vs a full chatting team), configured with extensive persona data, slang, pricing strategy, and trained on rapport/texting/selling scenarios; warns that a bad setup just creates 'a shitty chatter'.

variants.claim: The emerging chatting meta is a hybrid where AI handles new-subscriber volume and identifies real spenders, then hands those fans to human chatters for long-term girlfriend-experience retention.
variants.what_they_do: Has AI run all new subscribers and complete the first script to separate spenders from time-wasters, then moves real spenders to a human chatter who gives a daily girlfriend experience to a small client list (~20 fans each); reports ~70% unlock rate and a single whale spending $50k in 4 months.

variants.claim: AI chatbots are improving rapidly and will soon deliver low-latency, high-quality chat at a fraction of the cost of even the cheapest human VAs, making human chatting teams economically unviable.
variants.what_they_do: The consultant flags AI chat as an imminent market force that will undercut human chatters (including cheap Filipino VA teams) on both cost and latency, framing it as a structural shift rather than a distant future trend.

sources.quote: it only costs like $2 $300 a month, which is pennies compared to how much you're forking out... on chatters
if you don't set it up correctly, you are simply going to make a shitty chatter
I will let the AI deal with all the new subscribers... so that it can differentiate between the actual spenders and the time wasters
we want the chatter to specifically just focus on a specific number amount of clients

AI chat bots are making huge improvements very quickly and will soon become a dominant force in the market, delivering low latency, high quality chat at a fraction of the cost of even the cheapest of Filipino virtual assistants

palm_comparison.vs_us: Directly maps to Palm's active AI-chatting project (§8 build-toward): defines a concrete tool pattern and the heavy-configuration requirement.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Evaluate a configurable AI chat bot (Super Creator-class) for Palm's AI-chatting project, budgeting for heavy persona setup/training rather than expecting plug-and-play.

new_findings.title: Use an 'innocent yes-ladder' question to psychologically obligate the fan before sending a PPV

variants.claim: Asking 'Can I film a video for you, but I'm a bit shy — do you promise to go easy on me?' gets a yes-commitment that makes the fan feel obliged to purchase when the PPV arrives.
variants.what_they_do: Before sending any PPV, the chatter asks a softly framed question that (a) gets a 'yes' and (b) maintains the model's innocent persona. The two components are explicit: 'can I film for you' = the commitment; 'I'm a bit shy' = innocence framing. Once the fan agrees, the PPV is sent. The felt obligation drives conversion.

variants.claim: Framing PPV offers as time-limited and personally exclusive ('only for you') reliably increases conversion rates.
variants.what_they_do: Two distinct psychological levers are scripted: (1) urgency — 'only available for a few hours'; (2) exclusivity — 'I don't usually send this to just anyone but you're special.' Both are delivered conversationally so they feel organic, not like a sales blast.

sources.quote: Can I film a video for you, but I'm a bit shy? Do you promise you will go easy on me?
he feels obliged that he has to buy it because he already said yes to filming the video
make the offer feel exclusive and time sensitive example I have something special for you but it's only available for a few hours
I don't usually send this to just anyone but you're special I love talking to you

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal baseline (§8, outsourced). This is a specific pre-sale conversion mechanic not present in any Palm SOP.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Incorporate a scripted yes-ladder template into the in-house chatter playbook as the mandatory bridge between rapport and first PPV send. A/B test the exact phrasing ('can I film for you' vs. 'I made something for you') and track conversion rate per chatter.

new_findings.title: Three-stage follow-up protocol for unopened/unpurchased PPVs — guilt-trip only as last resort

variants.claim: When a fan doesn't buy, a structured follow-up sequence (curiosity ping → reason discovery → resolution → guilt-trip last resort) recovers sales without burning the relationship.
variants.what_they_do: Stage 1 — curiosity ping if left on seen: 'Where did you go?' / 'Don't just stare at it, you're making me nervous.' Stage 2 — if he replies but won't buy, ask why (trust issue, no money, bad timing) and resolve the specific objection (offer AI verification tip, suggest waiting). Stage 3 — only if he refuses to explain and is likely a time-waster: guilt-trip ('you made me film it and I'm not getting anything').

variants.claim: Sending a lone sad-face emoji to a subscriber who has gone quiet triggers guilt and reliably provokes a response, re-opening the monetization window.
variants.what_they_do: When a subscriber has not replied to a previous message, instead of sending follow-up texts or more content pitches, the chatter sends only a '😢' or '🥺' emoji. Bjorn claims ~99.9% response rate. The mechanism: guilt-trips the subscriber into explaining their absence, which restarts rapport and spending. No spamming — one emoji only.

variants.claim: Sending multiple unanswered messages in a row appears overwhelming and spammy, actively pushing subscribers away and reducing future engagement.
variants.what_they_do: The VA training guide explicitly prohibits sending additional messages if a subscriber has not responded. One message sits unanswered → chatter holds. The only permitted follow-up is the sad-face emoji tactic (single message). This is a firm SOP rule, not a suggestion.

sources.quote: If he left you on scene, you want to follow up, but don't be negative yet. Don't guilt trip. Use that as the last resort.
you want to find out why he isn't buying and resolve it

if a subscriber has not replied to your previous message and you want to get his attention simply just send a sad face emoji it is amazing how this will work I would say this probably works 99.9% of the time

do not spam subscribers with messages if they're not responding try not to send additional messages if the sub is not responding as this can appear overwhelming and spammy and could potentially put the subscriber off

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal baseline (§8, outsourced). Palm has no documented follow-up or objection-handling flow.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build this three-stage ladder into the in-house chatter SOP with explicit scripts for each stage. Flag time-wasters in the Fan Tracker (§9) after Stage 3 failure so chatters deprioritize them per the subscriber-priority queue (see finding on emoji tagging below).

new_findings.title: Loom-video SOPs to eliminate training misinterpretation

variants.claim: Recording yourself doing each task as a Loom video (rather than written docs) is the most reliable way to train hires and remove misinterpretation.
variants.what_they_do: Films a Loom once per task and reuses it forever; pairs Loom with backup documents for objections; uses live chatter monitoring on first 1-2 shifts plus 'trust but verify' checks for the first 1-2 weeks.

variants.claim: The fastest way to build an SOP library is to record a Loom of yourself doing the task, copy the auto-generated transcript into ChatGPT with a 'turn this into a step-by-step SOP' prompt, and paste the result directly into a categorized Notion SOP database.
variants.what_they_do: Loom.com recording → copy transcript → ChatGPT prompt → formatted SOP pasted into Notion SOP library, tagged by department (sales, client success, hiring, organic marketing, etc.) and status (draft/active). SOPs are linked from daily winning formula tasks and role onboarding courses via hyperlinks. Cultural rule: when a team member asks a question, don't answer it directly — create an SOP around it, send the SOP, and store it. The SOP library is filtered into role-specific views so each team member sees only their relevant SOPs.

sources.quote: Loom recordings
what we like to do is we like to use a tool called Loom, loom.com, and we just film a video on what we're doing. You go to the video, go to transcript, copy the transcript, turn this into a SOP
anytime somebody asks a question, what we're not doing is we're not just going to answer the question right from the jump. We're just going to create an SOP around it
these are all the SOPs. This is sales, client success, hiring, etc.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has SOP-style workflows in the editor/warmup UIs but no documented Loom-first training standard for new hires (§10). Cheap, high-leverage practice.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Standardize Loom-video SOPs for every Palm role (editor, poster, warmup operator, future chatters); store alongside the existing playbook editor.

new_findings.title: DM prospects from a personal-looking account, not an agency page

variants.claim: Run creator outreach from a personal-looking profile rather than a branded agency account so messages get read.
variants.what_they_do: Send from your personal/duplicated account (lifestyle pics, normal-person vibe), never an 'OFM manager' agency account, because models skim their request folder and skip anything that reads as an agency. Stop labeling yourself 'OFM manager' anywhere; call it 'talent/influencer management' instead so you aren't lumped in with the spam.

variants.claim: Setting up a credible Instagram profile with professional photos (not casual/topless) is the primary vehicle for cold-outreaching creators, and showing your face builds trust that converts to signed deals.
variants.what_they_do: Agency owner creates a personal IG account with polished location/lifestyle photos (real or Pinterest-sourced). Shows face for credibility; faceless is acceptable if IG photos are professional. DMs creators who appear stuck, plateaued, or post-bad-agency. Offers a free 1-week trial to prove value, then converts to 50/50. Moves promising leads to FaceTime to close.

sources.quote: most people send their messages out from like an agency account... I would actually highly advise you to do your personal account.
please stop saying OFM manager in either your username, your bio... try to be a little bit different... talent management agency.
you want to just make an Instagram profile, you want to set it up some nice business photos
you can offer if you find a model and you believe in yourself… I'll work for free for you for a week and if things improve then we can go to a 50/50 split

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm acquisition is Josh-DM-led + referrals with no documented outreach-profile standard (§6 THIN/GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Standardize an outreach profile spec (personal-style account, lifestyle content, non-'OFM' positioning) as part of Palm's acquisition playbook.

new_findings.topic: OF vault folder structure for chatter efficiency
new_findings.title: Organise OF vault into named script folders so chatters can locate PPV assets instantly

variants.claim: Structuring the OF content vault into named folders (e.g. 'Script 1', 'New Subscriber', 'PPV', 'SFW Feed') tied to specific chat scripts reduces chatter errors and speeds up PPV delivery during live conversations.
variants.what_they_do: Inside the OF vault the agency creates dedicated folders for: (1) each reusable sexting script (Script 1, Script 2, etc.) containing all media needed for that session; (2) a 'New Subscriber' folder with content specifically used in the new-sub welcome sequence; (3) a general 'PPV' folder; (4) optionally an 'SFW' folder for feed reposts. When a chatter replays a script with a new subscriber they navigate to the matching script folder to pull the correct assets in order.

variants.claim: Chatting drives 80%+ of OF revenue; chatters must have scripts with specific photos, videos, and voice notes pre-loaded so they can send PPV content in real time without hunting the vault mid-conversation.
variants.what_they_do: Agency builds a script library where each conversation branch has the relevant media asset already tagged/attached. Chatters follow the script and send the pre-matched content without interruption, maintaining flow and urgency in the conversation.

sources.quote: if you have big sexting scripts...you'll create folders in the vault called like script one script two Etc which is where all the content relating to that script will go
the easier you make it for them the less chance of mistakes there will be

80% or more should come from the chatting the messages and the tips so because that's the most important part you must have a system in place so you must have set scripts that you or if you've hired somebody to the to do the chatting they can follow now Within These scripts they should have pre-made content already there so photos and videos maybe even some voice notes

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's OF vault/PPV content system is THIN (§2) and chatting is currently outsourced (§8). There is no documented vault folder convention or script-to-asset mapping. This is directly relevant to Palm's roadmap item of building an in-house chat team and AI chatting system.
palm_comparison.recommendation: As build-toward for the in-house chat team: design the OF vault structure around scripts rather than raw chronology. Create a folder schema (New Subscriber, Script_01…Script_N, PPV_unlabelled, SFW_Feed) before handing off to chatters. Document which assets belong to which script in Airtable so the AI chatting system can programmatically select the right media during a conversation.

new_findings.title: Girlfriend-experience parasocial selling, not PPV spam

variants.claim: The girlfriend experience (parasocial connection, scripted live-feeling sells, no PPV spam) drives the highest and most durable chatting revenue.
variants.what_they_do: Optimizes the OF wall to feel like 'Snapchatting,' uses 7-10 stage scripts with verification images, never initiates sexual content (lets the fan initiate), handles broke fans with ego-protecting lines, and forbids PPV spamming across the whole team.

variants.claim: Subscribers don't spend money on OF because they can't find free explicit content; they spend because they're buying an emotional, intimate experience that free sites can't replicate.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are trained to frame every interaction as building genuine intimacy — shyness, excitement, escalating personal connection — rather than a content transaction. The chatter script is designed so the fan feels like the experience is happening *with* the creator, not being sold a product. The script's pacing (slow → fast) mirrors real intimacy. This psychological framing is positioned as the core reason scripted chats outperform both mass PPVs and one-off custom content.

sources.quote: the GF experience, the only thing that I try to run in OFM
only fans isn't about sexuality it's about emotions and experiences
what he really searches for are the emotions the real sexual experience not just the pure sexuality

palm_comparison.vs_us: Chatting is OUTSOURCED at Palm with no in-house SOP (§8). This is direct build-toward intelligence for Palm's planned in-house and AI chat teams.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Capture the GFE framework (wall optimization, staged scripts, verification, no-spam rule) as the seed SOP for Palm's future in-house/AI chatting.

new_findings.title: Five-tier chat priority queue: active sext session first, time-wasters last

variants.claim: Organizing the subscriber inbox into five explicit priority tiers ensures chatters always work highest-revenue conversations first and never waste time on non-spenders while whales wait.
variants.what_they_do: Priority order: (1) fans currently in a sext/purchase session, (2) fans being transitioned into a session (teasing phase), (3) new subscribers (unknown spend potential — test them), (4) known spenders/whales in relationship-building, (5) time-wasters — only addressed when inbox is empty. Agency adjusts exact ordering based on where their revenue concentrates.

variants.claim: When managing multiple accounts simultaneously, chatters should prioritize responses to high-spending or long-tenure subscribers over the general queue.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters explicitly rank their open conversations: whales and loyal long-term members get first responses. Low-spend or new subscribers are handled after, often with templated messages to maintain efficiency without abandoning them.

sources.quote: number one in my opinion for everybody should be the ones that are currently in a sexting session
The last ones should be time wasters because they're just wasting our time
prioritize high value chats focus on subscribers who frequently spend money or longtime members

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a whale-hunting module (§9) and Fan Tracker CRM but no documented real-time chat prioritization queue. The outsourced chat team may have their own system, but Palm has no visibility into it.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When building the in-house chat team, implement this queue as a visible status layer in the Fan Tracker CRM. Consider surfacing active-session and new-subscriber flags in the whale-hunting dashboard (§9) so the chat manager can monitor live priority distribution across creators.

new_findings.topic: KPI tracking and A/B testing culture
new_findings.title: Track all agency KPIs and A/B test every variable before committing to it

variants.claim: Knowing your exact PPV purchase rate, chatting ratio, average reply time, Instagram conversion, views-per-sub, and LTV — and A/B testing every script or tactic in 3+ variants — is the operational discipline that separates scaling agencies from stuck ones.
variants.what_they_do: Agency maintains a live spreadsheet where the owner can quote every core metric from memory or within 2 minutes. For any new script or tactic, they run 3 simultaneous variants across chatters, track unlock/purchase rates per variant, identify the winning element (e.g., a different opening angle), and roll that element into all other scripts. Testing is weekly and continuous.

variants.claim: A single visual Miro board showing all employees with ratings, target revenue, timeline progress, current revenue, and per-creator ratios makes every bottleneck visible and creates a clear step-by-step operating orientation for the agency owner.
variants.what_they_do: Griffin builds and shares a Miro board with each student/client. The board contains: all team members with a performance rating next to each, target revenue goal, timeline to hit it, current revenue, and per-model revenue ratios. It is described as 'basic tracking' — not pulling granular IG KPIs — but the visual format makes bottlenecks immediately apparent. It is credited as the primary driver of Barry's jump from $80K to $100K/mo.

sources.quote: what's your PPV purchase rate, what's your chatting ratio, your average reply time, your Instagram conversion, how many views do you need to make per sub
you built this entire like visual map of my agency that I've never seen in that way every bottleneck was just visible right there on the screen
we have every single one of your employees with a rating next to it the target revenue the progress to the timeline your current revenue your ratios for each model

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has partial analytics infrastructure: the Whale Hunting dashboard and Fan Tracker CRM (§9 — PARTIAL) capture fan-side data, and the content pipeline tracks viral scores (§1 — MATURE). However, there is no documented agency-level KPI dashboard covering PPV purchase rate, chatter reply time, or IG conversion (§7 — GAP, §9 — GAP). A/B testing of chatting scripts is not possible today because chatting is outsourced (§8 — OUTSOURCED).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a single 'Agency Health' Airtable view or admin dashboard page tracking at minimum: IG follower growth rate, IG-to-OF conversion %, OF subscriber count, PPV purchase rate, and revenue per creator. As the in-house chat team is built (§8 roadmap), wire A/B test tracking into chatter SOP from day one — log script variant, message count, and revenue outcome per conversation.

new_findings.title: Hire chatters on flat rate, commission, or hybrid — reinvest first revenue

variants.claim: Once initial revenue is generated, the first reinvestment should be hiring chatters on a flat rate, small commission, or hybrid comp structure to free up operator time for scaling.
variants.what_they_do: Describes a reinvestment-first scaling loop: operator chats manually → earns first revenue → hires chatters (flat fee or commission or both) → reclaims time → signs more clients → hires more chatters → repeats. The comp structure is flexible (flat/commission/hybrid) rather than a fixed model.

variants.claim: The cost-effective chatting model is hiring low-cost offshore chatters and de-risking them through heavy recurring training plus fully systemized scripts so output doesn't depend on their prior experience.
variants.what_they_do: Hires Filipino chatters at ~$2-3/hour via OnlineJobs.ph, distrusts 'experienced' chatters who spam PPVs, and trains them personally 4-5 times a week (~20-30 min) on his own systems so every chatter sounds identical and 'can't mess up'.

sources.quote: you can then reinvest it and you can hire what are called chatters. These are people who handle all of the DMs for you, either for a flat rate or a small commission or a mixture of both.
Go to online jobs.ph. Get yourself a Filipino chatter. Two $3 per hour
teaching your chatters at least three times per week for 30 minutes a day minimum

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is fully outsourced (§8) so this loop is bypassed at the current stage. However, Palm has no documented chatter comp model, hiring criteria, or onboarding process for when it builds an in-house team. This is a build-toward gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Begin documenting chatter comp benchmarks now (flat rate ranges, commission % norms, hybrid structures) sourced from competitor content like this. Build a chatter job description and scoring rubric ahead of the in-house chat team launch. This prevents scrambling when the timeline moves up.

new_findings.title: Never pause acquisition — run it as a 24/7 autopilot system

variants.claim: The single biggest lever that broke Ellis past $30K/month was an acquisition system that never stops, not better marketing or chatting.
variants.what_they_do: Ellis runs a three-component acquisition machine (lead-magnet content + paid ads + a specific outreach method) combined with VSLs, a funnel, a call-setting service, and a closer — all running simultaneously and never paused even when onboarding a new creator. The system converts models continuously without founder involvement.

variants.claim: Outreach should be tracked stage-by-stage in a daily spreadsheet so the bleeding step is fixed one variable at a time after a meaningful sample.
variants.what_they_do: Logs source link, DMs sent, replies, qualified convos, asks-for-call, booked/showed/closed per channel; waits ~3,000 DMs before changing anything, then changes one variable at a time. Uses a 7-12 message value-first follow-up chain (most deals close on touch 5-7) including a 'red flag' breakup follow-up with ~95% response.

sources.quote: the only thing that took us past it, it wasn't marketing, it wasn't chatting, it was getting a solid acquisition system that never stops running
after about 3,000 DMs... you'll know which layer of your system is actually bleeding
Most deals happen between the fifth and seventh touch point

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is Josh-DM-led and effectively pauses when attention shifts to delivery (§6). There is no documented funnel, VSL, or call-setting layer, and acquisition is not yet automated or delegated.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document Palm's current outreach method into an SOP, then hire/delegate outreach execution so acquisition runs independently of delivery. Add a funnel layer (lead-magnet video → VSL → booked call) to qualify higher-tier creators before the call. Treat acquisition as an always-on background process, not a sequential phase.

new_findings.topic: Reddit and X as supplementary traffic channels
new_findings.title: Contract out Reddit and X content distribution as a low-overhead traffic stream

variants.claim: Outsourcing Reddit and X content distribution to a specialist contractor can yield disproportionate revenue relative to cost (cited: $1K/month spend generating ~$20K/month in revenue).
variants.what_they_do: Agency pays a Reddit/X specialist contractor roughly $1,000/month to distribute content across relevant subreddits and X accounts, funneling traffic to OF. The contractor is found through the agency's Telegram network. ROI is tracked as revenue attributed to that traffic source versus contractor cost.

variants.claim: Agencies still spending on Reddit, paid ads, SFS, and GG swaps are watching already-thin margins deteriorate and should reallocate entirely to organic social.
variants.what_they_do: The consultant explicitly names Reddit spend, paid advertising, SFS (shoutout-for-shoutout), and GG (girl-girl) swaps as legacy tactics that are now net-negative when factoring in declining ROI and margin compression, advocating a full pivot to organic social content.

sources.quote: I pay him like a thousand bucks a month and he brings me like $20,000 a month
your Reddit and X contractors are distributing content and funneling into loads of subs turning into hefty profit
Many agencies are still using the old methods, throwing money at Reddit and paid ads and SFS and GG swaps, and their already razor thin margins are getting worse by the day

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented Reddit or X traffic strategy (§5 GAP). This is a pure learn-from-others gap that applies to real creators and is relatively easy to test at low cost.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Source a Reddit/X contractor via OFM Telegram groups. Start with a 30-day paid pilot (~$500–$1K) for one creator, using UTM-tagged links or OF referral tracking to attribute revenue. If 5x+ ROI is confirmed, formalize as a recurring line item per creator.

new_findings.title: Keep OF link off satellite accounts — funnel to intermediate IG pages only

variants.claim: Placing the OF link only on the main creator page (not on satellite/funnel accounts) reduces satellite account suspension rate to approximately 1-in-20.
variants.what_they_do: Satellite accounts never carry any OF or external link in their bios. Their sole job is to drive IG profile visits to the creator's main account (or a set of 5 intermediate accounts). The OF link lives exclusively at the final destination page. This separation keeps satellite accounts off Meta's adult-content radar and dramatically lowers ban rates.

variants.claim: Because AI model accounts are vulnerable to Instagram takedowns, you protect compound growth by keeping the main posting account clean and pushing the monetization link through a separate 'slave' account tagged in the bio.
variants.what_they_do: Main (mother) account posts content normally, builds followers and watch time, and tags a second 'slave' account in bio. The slave account holds the link-in-bio to Fanvue/OF. When Instagram removes the slave account (expected), the mother account survives and keeps its audience; a new slave is swapped in. Real human creators avoid this because they're ID-verified and harder to ban.

sources.quote: in none of these accounts' bios, we're putting that link... we're just funneling to other Instagram pages, so our accounts can stay way safer
we're going to take our models on a second account and then put that in bio
This is the mother account, main account, and then the slave account is where we take it at

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's current documented setup (§5) routes traffic through a single creator IG → link-in-bio → OF. The satellite layer with link-segregation is not present. Palm's AI persona accounts do use Beacons link-in-bio (§3), which may face similar flagging risk if OF is referenced. This architecture principle — isolate the OF link to as few accounts as possible — is directly applicable.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Audit current Palm creator IG setups: does every account that references OF carry the link? If so, consider a two-tier architecture where traffic-generation accounts funnel to a single 'conversion' account that holds the OF link. This reduces surface area for platform enforcement actions across the entire portfolio.

new_findings.title: Systemise the OF backend before scaling traffic — uncontrolled sub inflow breaks unprepared teams

variants.claim: An unsystemised OF backend (no tracking links, no per-creator content plan, inconsistent handling) will collapse under sudden high sub volume, wasting the traffic wins.
variants.what_they_do: Ellis describes an incident where a creator brought in 3,500 subs overnight and the agency had to pull chatters from other models, scramble chat managers, and operate chaotically. His prescribed fix: implement tracking links per traffic source, standardise how every creator's page is managed (content plan, PPV plan, data review), and ensure every process has an SOP so any new chatter/manager can be slotted in without handholding.

variants.claim: An agency must have more operational infrastructure than its current client load requires at all times, so any inbound creator can be onboarded immediately without dropping balls on existing clients.
variants.what_they_do: The speaker frames this as a readiness posture: 'if I know that any given week there might be a creator that reaches out that I'm really interested in, I need to have more infrastructure than needed for my current client base.' Reactive hiring after signing = delayed onboarding, overwhelmed ops, damaged existing accounts, and lost creator trust. The prescription is to treat hiring/staffing as a proactive capacity investment, not a response to demand.

sources.quote: you have no tracking mechanism — you're probably not using tracking links to see where the traffic's coming from, and every single creator is getting handled in a different way
you actually need to have a backend infrastructure ready to take on more profiles before those profiles even are coming to you

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's OF vault/PPV backend is THIN (§2) with no documented standardisation of per-creator page management or tracking links. Chat is outsourced (§8) which partially insulates Palm from the chaos scenario, but the lack of standardised backend setup means scaling a creator's traffic still risks operational breakdown.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Before aggressively scaling any creator's traffic, complete a backend readiness checklist: (1) tracking links live per platform, (2) PPV content plan drafted, (3) chatter capacity confirmed with external team, (4) escalation SOP defined. This is also foundational groundwork for the in-house chat roadmap (§8 build-toward).

new_findings.title: Start with exactly 2 clients — not 1, not 3+

variants.claim: New agencies should aim to sign two clients simultaneously: one as a hedge if the other underperforms, but no more than two to avoid operational overwhelm before systems are proven.
variants.what_they_do: Prescribes a deliberate two-client start: if one relationship fails the other provides continuity, while capping at two prevents the operator from over-extending before they have SOPs, chatters, or reliable traffic in place.

variants.claim: Beginners who try to sign many clients simultaneously disappoint everyone and burn out; mastering operations on one client first produces better outcomes.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan explicitly advises new agency operators to sign a single creator, test all marketing and chatting strategies on that one account, and only expand once they know what works. He argues that without in-house chatters (which beginners can't afford) and without proven marketing, having five-plus clients is unmanageable and leads to poor results across the board.

sources.quote: you should ideally aim to sign two clients from the get-go. If you just have one, you can stay with one, of course, but in most cases, one of them might not work out
focus on one client to begin with because a lot of beginners will try and just like scatter gun and try and get as many clients as they can thinking that that is going to solve their problems

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented guidance on optimal portfolio size at different stages of agency maturity (§6 GAP). No scaling model or client-count-to-capacity ratio is written down.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define explicit capacity tiers in Palm's onboarding/ops documentation: e.g., Tier 0 (founding) = 1-2 creators, Tier 1 = up to 5 once chatting is delegated, Tier 2 = 10+ once a full team is in place. Use this to gate new creator signings against current team bandwidth.

new_findings.title: PPV Anchors: Plant a story or fantasy 6–24 hours before sending the PPV to manufacture perceived relevance

variants.claim: Setting up a PPV in advance — by mentioning a dream, fantasy, new outfit, or story — gives chatters a credible, personalized reason for sending the video later, making it seem far more valuable than a cold send.
variants.what_they_do: Chatter plants an anchor in DMs (e.g. 'I just had the craziest dream about doing XYZ with you in my green lingerie') or on the feed (e.g. 'just got this new outfit, what do you think?' with a photo). They do NOT sell immediately. They return 6, 12, or 24 hours later referencing the anchor ('I couldn't help but make that dream a reality — I put on that green lingerie and…') and send the PPV. The delay is what makes it feel authentic rather than scripted. Feed-level anchors (outfit post, emotional story post) can hit many subs simultaneously, with chatters following up individually in DMs.

variants.claim: Rather than describing the video's explicit content directly (which sounds repetitive and generic), chatters sext about an unrelated fantasy the creator 'has been having,' escalate it fully, then link the PPV as what the creator made while thinking about that fantasy.
variants.what_they_do: Chatter chooses a fantasy (threesome, free use, femdom, cheating, etc.) — ideally one the subscriber has shown interest in — and opens with 'there's something I've been thinking about but I'm embarrassed to say.' They build out the fantasy in full descriptive detail, getting the sub emotionally invested. When the sub is clearly engaged, the chatter pitches the PPV: 'I made this video thinking about doing that for the first time.' They connect specific moments in the video to the sexting ('at 2:41 I was thinking about what you said about…'). Fantasies are ranked from least to most explicit; each PPV in the loop uses the next fantasy up the ladder. This gives chatters an inexhaustible, varied pitch mechanism that doesn't repeat.

sources.quote: we can set up pay-per-views in advanced in the DMs or on the main feed... this should act as lore for the pay-per-view, which gives you a reason for sending it later
that delay between when you bring up the anchor and when you go into sell it is what makes it seem authentic
an alternative to that is sexting as if you were opening up about a fantasy that the creator has been having
you keep talking about the fantasy until they're clearly into it, and then you link that to the next pay-per-view saying something like, I made this video thinking about doing that for the first time

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal SOP for pre-seeding PPV context exists (§8 outsourced, §2 thin on PPV vault strategy). Palm does have a content posting pipeline (§1) and scheduling infrastructure (§5), which could be used to coordinate feed-level anchors with chatter follow-up timing.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a 'PPV anchor' protocol into the future in-house chat SOP: define anchor types (DM-personal vs feed-broadcast), specify the delay window (6–24 hrs), and create a coordination mechanism between the posting schedule (Publer/Telegram) and the chat team so chatters know when an anchor post goes live and can follow up. This is a high-leverage tactic because it uses existing content infrastructure.

new_findings.title: Live-call training with seconds-fast feedback loops and single-point focus

variants.claim: The fastest way to improve a new chatter is to sit on a live call with them while they chat, give feedback within seconds, and focus on fixing only one thing at a time.
variants.what_they_do: Manager joins a live call with the chatter while they work in real time. Feedback is immediate ('stop, do it again'). Manager identifies the single highest-leverage improvement point and works only on that until fixed, then moves to the next. Chatters are not 'saved' — manager tells them what to do but makes them execute it themselves so the learning sticks.

variants.claim: An open call for all chatters currently on shift — combined with assigning subject-matter 'authorities' among chatters — creates collaborative learning that makes the whole team improve faster than top-down coaching alone.
variants.what_they_do: Maintain an always-open call channel for chatters on the same shift so they can ask each other for help and share mistakes in real time. Identify each chatter's main strength (e.g., scripting, PPV captioning). When another chatter needs help in that area, route them to the peer expert rather than the manager. This builds internal figures of authority and reduces manager bottleneck.

sources.quote: Fast feedback loops. If you want someone to learn something quickly, you need there to be a short gap between when they do something and when they get feedback on it. And I mean seconds
Don't step in and do it for them. You can tell them what to do. That's how they actually learn
having an on shift call where the chatters who are currently working can talk to each other
Chatters tend to have one main strength. Identify what that is and then whenever a different chatter asks for help on that topic, you send them to that chatter

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal chatter training exists (§8, outsourced). Build-toward.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt this as the core training protocol for first 2–4 weeks of any new in-house chatter. Build a simple tracker: one 'current focus point' per chatter updated weekly so managers don't multi-task the coaching.

new_findings.topic: TikTok geo-targeting from outside the US
new_findings.title: Use US SIM card + US-based uploader account to target US TikTok audience from abroad

variants.claim: To reliably serve content to a US TikTok audience when the creator or agency is based outside the US, the account must be managed by someone physically in the US using a US SIM, and the creator must never log into the same TikTok account from a non-US location.
variants.what_they_do: Three-step approach: (1) order a US SIM card and insert it into the posting device; (2) optionally use a US VPN; (3) best option — hire a US-based person to log into and upload from the creator's TikTok account. Strict rule: once a US-based operator is running the account, the creator outside the US must never log in, or it corrupts the geo-signal.

variants.claim: If a creator posts TikTok from a country with weak purchasing power (e.g., Colombia), viral reach fills her funnel with followers who cannot afford OF subscriptions — a US SIM card or US proxy poster fixes the audience geography.
variants.what_they_do: For creators based in Latin America or other non-Tier-1 countries, agency either ships/provides a US SIM card so the creator's device registers as US-based, or recruits a US-based person to post on her behalf, ensuring TikTok's algorithm surfaces content to US audiences.

sources.quote: us SIM card will help so if you order a US SIM card it'll give you a different bunch of different ideas right
find some in the US who lives there pay him to upload the content for you make sure your model does not log into the tick tock because she does then it messes it all up

you would not really my advice would not be for you to get her to post it herself on Tik Tok because you'll if she goes viral she'll get a bunch of Colombian followers and the currency exchange doesn't really make sense

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's posting/distribution strategy is not fully documented (§5 GAP). Palm uses Publer and Amin (contractor) for scheduling/posting but no geo-targeting protocol for TikTok is noted. Palm's AI personas (§3) use hardware-isolated GrapheneOS devices with dedicated SIMs — the SIM-per-account logic here directly mirrors that infrastructure and could be extended to geo-targeted TikTok posting.
palm_comparison.recommendation: For any Palm creator (real or AI) targeting the US market from a non-US base, implement the US-SIM device rule. Given Palm already has a SIM inventory system (§3/§4), extend it to include a 'geo: US' SIM pool for TikTok posting devices. Document the hard rule: one geo per account, creator never logs in from a conflicting location once a remote poster is active. Assign this to the Phase 3 distribution build (§5).

new_findings.topic: Client wins database as retention and sales ammo
new_findings.title: Maintain a running 'wins database' — screenshots, metrics, clips — per client for retention and resigning

variants.claim: Systematically capturing and storing client wins (lead screenshots, view counts, clips) in a tagged database gives the CSM live ammunition to counter 'are you doing anything for me?' objections, improves retention at renewal, and provides case study material for new sales.
variants.what_they_do: Notion 'wins' database with fields: win type (leads/views/revenue/video performance), client relation, screenshot or media URL, date. Anyone on the team can add a win. KPI: minimum X wins per client per month set as a CSM metric. Wins roll up to client OS so the CSM can open a client record and instantly see a count and list of wins. Used on retention calls, renewals, and sales calls. Goal: ~20+ wins/month company-wide as a tracked number on the client success dashboard.

sources.quote: we want to create a culture at our company that we collect wins, we celebrate wins, and we stack wins. We have almost a thousand wins of just screenshots
I would try to get like at least a couple of wins a month for each client and make that like your KPI internally from like a client success perspective
when you're on a call with a client and they're like, 'Oh, I'm just not really seeing anything.' It's like, 'Dude, like we've gotten you 10 leads.'

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has whale-hunting and Fan Tracker tools (§9) for fan-level data, but no documented system for capturing and storing creator wins (viral posts, subscriber milestones, revenue records) as a retention tool. The §9 gap also notes no written retention strategy. A wins database is directly applicable: Palm's editors and social team observe creator wins daily and could log them systematically.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'Creator Wins' table in Airtable (Content Ops or HQ base) with fields: creator, win type (milestone sub count, viral reel, PPV revenue record, press mention), media URL, date. Make logging a win a required step for editors/social team when they observe a notable metric. Josh or a creator success role reviews and shares wins back to creators weekly — this doubles as creator retention messaging and provides case study content for acquisition.

new_findings.topic: Cloud phone farm for IG account amplification
new_findings.title: Operate 200–300 cloud-based IG accounts to funnel traffic to creator main pages

variants.claim: Running hundreds of warmed-up Instagram accounts via cloud phones on automated schedules generates 50–200 additional subs per day per creator while insulating the main account from bans.
variants.what_they_do: Agency spins up 200–300 Instagram accounts on cloud-hosted real phones. Automations handle: feed scrolling, liking, Reel posting, phone-number verification — all to mimic organic human behavior and avoid suspensions. None of these satellite accounts carry the OF link in bio; they funnel only to the creator's main IG page (or to 5 intermediate accounts). The OF link lives only on the main page. Suspension rate drops to ~1-in-20 vs. higher rates when the OF link is in satellite bios. If main account is banned, warmed satellite accounts are handed to the creator to restart on.

sources.quote: we set up hundreds of Instagram accounts... you can run everything off the cloud
In none of these accounts' bios, we're putting that link... we're just funneling to other Instagram pages
if we have 200 phones for one creator, and we're literally pulling one subscriber per day from each of the phones, then that's 200 subs a day

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a mature AI-account warmup system for AI personas (§3/§4) using GrapheneOS Pixels and Publer, but this is for synthetic creator accounts, not a satellite-funnel network for real creators. Palm has no documented equivalent for real-creator traffic amplification (§4 🔲 GAP — real-creator warmup/cadence not formalized; §5 🔲 GAP — traffic strategy not documented).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Evaluate adopting a scaled cloud-phone funnel for real creators. Palm's existing hardware-isolated warmup infrastructure (GrapheneOS, SIM inventory, Publer) gives a meaningful head start — the playbook could be adapted. Key decisions: (1) cloud phone provider selection, (2) automation tooling for feed warm-up, (3) funnel topology (satellite → main IG → OF). Start small (20–30 accounts for one creator) before scaling to 200+. Flag as high-priority given current IG instability signals.

new_findings.topic: Chatting ratio KPI and chatter hire threshold
new_findings.title: Target 1:10 sub-to-chat revenue ratio; hire chatters only after reaching $2–5K/month agency revenue

variants.claim: A 1:10 ratio of subscription revenue to DM/tip revenue is the core chatting KPI, and chatters should not be hired until the agency earns $2–5K/month to ensure they're affordable.
variants.what_they_do: Chatting ratio defined as: for every $1 in subscription revenue, target $10 in DM+tip revenue. Benchmarks: 1:6 = decent, 1:8 = good, 1:10 = great, 1:15 = excellent. Hire VA chatters at $3–$5/hour + commission once agency hits $2–5K/month. Manage chatters via Discord. Welcome message framework: thank sub, ask their name, build rapport → send teasers → sell PPVs → upsell. Chatting is treated as a strategy, not ad-hoc.

variants.claim: Chatting performance should be measured as a ratio of message/tip revenue to subscription revenue, with elite teams targeting roughly 1:9 to 1:10.
variants.what_they_do: Calculates (messages + tips) / subscriptions; reports turning a ~1:1 ratio creator into 1:10+, and uses the ratio gap on creator calls to show lost revenue (e.g. a 1:3 vs 1:9 difference of thousands/month).

sources.quote: for every $1 you have in the chats, you want to have $10 in the or in the subs. You want to have $10 in the chats. So we stick to 1 to 10
once you're paying or making 2 to 5K a month, hire a VA to chat. So, if you're making, you know, 1K a month, 700 bucks a month, do not hire chatters
add up your messages and tips as well and divide it by your subscriptions

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is fully outsourced (§8) with no internal KPI, ratio target, or hire-threshold documented. This is directly relevant to Palm's ~1yr roadmap to bring chatting in-house and the active AI chatting project. The 1:10 ratio is a concrete benchmark Palm should adopt as a performance target for the external team today and the in-house team when built.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Immediately: share the 1:10 sub-to-chat ratio benchmark with the external chat team as a performance SLA and track it in the existing Fan Tracker / whale-hunting infrastructure (§9). Build-toward: use the $3–$5/hour + commission pay structure and Discord management model as the template for in-house chatter hiring. Add 'chatting ratio' as a tracked metric in the Airtable reporting layer.

new_findings.title: Identify and solve one operational bottleneck at a time rather than fixing everything at once

variants.claim: Agencies scale most effectively by diagnosing the single largest bottleneck — social coverage, chatter quality, chatter hours — and solving only that before moving to the next.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan walks through a sequential bottleneck-resolution framework: (1) Could more social accounts drive more revenue? Yes → outsource social. (2) Could 24/7 chatting earn more? Yes → hire chatters. (3) Could better-trained chatters earn more? Yes → build chatter training. Each step is solved fully before the next is attempted. He explicitly warns against trying to solve all problems simultaneously, which leads to overwhelm and paralysis.

variants.claim: Agencies that spread effort across many marketing channels become mediocre at all of them; depth in one channel outperforms breadth across many.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan's second top lesson: pick one traffic/marketing method and become the best at it before expanding. He acknowledges the risk of single-channel dependency (e.g., TikTok declining) but frames transferable skills as the safety net — skills learned on TikTok apply to Instagram Reels or YouTube Shorts. The discipline is still to go deep on one channel first, then adapt skills to adjacent channels when the primary shows diminishing returns.

sources.quote: look at where your bottlenecks are in your business and work from there so you know could you make more money with a client if you had more social media accounts running traffic yes okay well how do you do that
lesson two is always going to go back to marketing so many people are trying to do everything yeah just focus on one thing and become the best at it

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm is actively running multiple parallel initiatives (SMM hub redesign §11, AI chatting project §8, warmup improvements §4, Phase 3 Publer §5). The risk Dylan describes — solving everything at once — is live. Palm does not have a documented prioritization framework for which bottleneck to tackle first.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt a formal bottleneck log in Airtable or the admin portal: a ranked list of operational constraints with a single 'active focus' flag. At each leadership sync, confirm only one bottleneck is in active resolution. This isn't about slowing down — it's about ensuring each fix is fully deployed and measured before the next one starts. Apply this now to sequence the SMM consolidation, chat buildout, and Phase 3 Publer work.

new_findings.title: A cheap competitive model wins on marketing, not on max realism

variants.claim: Operators stress that earnings come from marketing, niching and sales rather than from having the most photorealistic model, and a sub-$100 toolchain is enough to compete.
variants.what_they_do: An 'intermediate' workflow (base model + reference set + ChatGPT-generated prompt bank + skin-enhancer for pores/flaws, under $100) is presented as the banker that made five figures; advanced ComfyUI/outsourced-developer models are optional for those with cash, but 'it isn't the quality of your model, it's the marketing, the sales.'

sources.quote: this whole workflow you can set up for under $100
It isn't the quality of your model. It's the marketing, the sales

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's strength is data-driven content ideation (§1 MATURE) and a hardware-isolated AI-persona program (§3); model-generation toolchain economics are relevant only to the future AI line.
palm_comparison.recommendation: For the future AI line, budget a lean generation toolchain and weight investment toward marketing/niching over chasing maximal realism; keep ComfyUI/dev-grade models for flagship personas only.

new_findings.title: Set expectations via call + concise video/docs emphasizing common beginner mistakes first

variants.claim: New chatters must be shown explicitly what 'good' looks like via a call plus a short video and documents that lead with the most common beginner errors before anything else.
variants.what_they_do: Run an onboarding call with the new chatter; share a pre-recorded video and 2–3 concise documents. Documents are structured around the most common mistakes (dry replies that kill conversation, overly aggressive/obvious PPV transitions) and use side-by-side examples — 'wrong version' vs 'right version' — for carrying conversations, captioning PPVs, transitioning, etc.

sources.quote: We put emphasis on common beginner mistakes
examples are king in your documents. Just show this is an example of a chatter doing it wrong. This is an example of a chatter doing it right

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal chatter onboarding materials exist at Palm (§8, outsourced). Build-toward for in-house team.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Create a 'chatter starter kit': a ≤10-min Loom walkthrough + a 1-page doc listing top 5 beginner mistakes with example chat screenshots. Prioritize mistake-first framing over comprehensive rules.

new_findings.topic: Inbound-pull DM strategy vs. mass PPV blasting
new_findings.title: Use feed posts as DM bait to pull fans inbound rather than blasting PPV

variants.claim: Posting a simple 'I'm online / bored' caption on the creator's feed draws fans into DMs on their own initiative, giving the chatter frame control and avoiding the 'spammy' feel of mass PPV messages.
variants.what_they_do: Instead of mass-sending PPV blasts, the chatter (or creator post) publishes a low-key feed post ('hey I'm up, who's up?', 'come to my DMs right now') that signals availability without directly soliciting money. Fans who respond have self-selected as engaged; the chatter is now in a reactive, high-authority position to run the upsell sequence.

sources.quote: post on your page say hey I'm online hey I'm bored um just post a picture caption it and they'll get the idea that you're online
you don't want to ever spam with ppv this is a huge No-No it cheapens your brand

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented DM-pull strategy or mass-message cadence guidance (§8 outsourced, §5 GAP on traffic/distribution strategy). This inbound-pull framing is a complementary tactic to whatever the external chat team is doing and is relevant for the in-house roadmap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Incorporate 'availability bait' posts as a scheduled content type in the Publer/Airtable pipeline — short captions designed to trigger DM inflow, not tied to a content asset. Brief the external chat team on this so they're primed when the flood comes, and document it as a standard practice for the in-house chat SOP.

new_findings.topic: Commonality building as the core sales engine
new_findings.title: Three-tier commonality system (me-too / mutual contact / tangential connection) to manufacture emotional bond

variants.claim: Building plausible commonalities with subscribers — using a tiered system from direct 'me too' to loose tangential connections — is the single most important driver of repeat purchasing.
variants.what_they_do: After qualification, chatters build commonalities using three levels: Level 1 ('me too') — chatter claims to share the same trait directly (e.g., 'I also study psychology'). Used sparingly because it's hard to track across many subs. Level 2 ('I know someone who') — chatter links via a third party ('My little brother studies psychology'). Common but overuse feels weird. Level 3 (tangential connection) — chatter links the sub's trait to something loosely related ('I read a Harvard Business Review article about students who study under 2 hours a night…'). This is the core technique — it's harder to fake, opens new conversational threads, and doesn't require the chatter to claim personal overlap. The goal is to give a 'plausible reason' for emotional connection so that future sales requests don't feel transactional.

sources.quote: building commonalities this is going to be the most important section in this presentation
oh really that's interesting I was reading an article in the Harvard Business review and it said that students who study under 2 hours a night do 50% worse

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal baseline at Palm (§8, outsourced). Build-toward. Notably, the 'plausible reason for validation' logic is essentially applied sales psychology that Palm could encode as a training principle, not just a chatting script.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Make Level 3 commonality building the centrepiece of Palm's in-house chatter training. Create a worked-example library organized by profession/hobby (e.g., 'if sub says he's a nurse, here are three Level 3 connections'). Pair this with the qualification CRM data so chatters can prep connections before re-engaging a returning sub.

new_findings.title: Write immersive, boner-test PPV descriptions that sell the fantasy before the sub sees the content

variants.claim: Long, vivid PPV descriptions that pass a 'can you picture it with your eyes closed' imagination test and a 'does it arouse' boner test dramatically increase unlock rates because the sub is buying stimulation from the description itself, not the preview.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are required to write out full descriptions for every PPV — not 'here's a pic of my boobs babe' but a multi-sentence, immersive description of camera angle, positioning, what the model is doing, and what the sub should imagine. Two quality tests: (1) Imagination test — can someone visualize exactly what is in the content just from the words, with no preview? (2) Boner test — does the description itself arouse? If a male chatter reads it and finds it hot, it will likely work. Pre-writing descriptions for an entire set before the selling session begins is standard practice. This is treated as a core skill, not an afterthought.

sources.quote: they are not vivid they do not stimulate the subscriber's imagination
does the description vividly describe what is in the content… will the description give the sub a boner when they read it

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no internal PPV copywriting standard (§2 THIN, §7 GAP). This is directly actionable: even before building an in-house chat team, Palm could create a PPV description template and writing guide for the external chat team and for creator-facing content briefing.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Create a PPV description writing guide with the two-test framework (imagination + boner test) and worked examples contrasting bad ('here's a pic babe') vs. good (fully immersive). Deliver this to the external chat team now as a quick win. When building the in-house team, make PPV description writing a standalone scored exercise in the pre-training filter — candidates who can't write a vivid description after coaching should not progress.

new_findings.title: Chatting is now commoditized — compete on front-end marketing, not back-end chat

variants.claim: Chatting has been commoditized to revshare deals as low as 5%, so agencies can no longer differentiate on chat quality and must compete on organic social marketing instead.
variants.what_they_do: This consultant advises agencies to deprioritize chatting as a differentiator and instead invest heavily in organic social content production as the primary value proposition to creators — arguing that front-end traffic generation is now the only defensible competitive moat.

sources.quote: Chatting has now become completely commoditized. Today, just about every agency understands the core principles of good chat and has a fundamentally identical offer
in 2026 and beyond, the greatest value you can possibly offer a new model is on the front end

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm already outsources chatting (§8) and has its strongest system in organic social content production (§1), which aligns directly with this thesis. However, Palm has not explicitly framed its organic social strength as the core competitive differentiator when pitching/signing creators (§6 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Incorporate this framing explicitly into creator acquisition pitches: lead with Palm's data-driven content engine (§1) as the primary value-add, not chat. When building the in-house chat roadmap (§8), treat it as table-stakes infrastructure, not a differentiator. Document this positioning in the acquisition SOP.

new_findings.title: Hire one operations person per 10–20 creators to handle client comms and daily duties

variants.claim: A practical delegation trigger for a horizontal agency is one account manager hired for every 10–20 creators, focused purely on client communication and routine tasks.
variants.what_they_do: Hunter's explicit hiring cadence: start solo, outsource chatting/content to a chatting agency immediately, hire first internal person when workload is unmanageable (focused on creator comms and daily duties), then scale at 1 hire per 10–20 creators. At 16 creators with in-house chatting, he calculates ~48 chatters (3 per creator) plus 4+ managers for oversight pods of 10.

sources.quote: Once I surpass the 10 model mark, I'm going to start hiring one person for every 10 models to keep up with the client communication and all the duties that they need to take on.
You need about 48 people that you need to manage. Of those 48 people, you're going to need pods of 10 with management.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's org is currently Josh + Evan + Amin + editors + external chat team (§10) with no documented hiring trigger, org scaling plan, or headcount ratios. §10 GAP on org chart and scaling plan.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define Palm's hiring trigger thresholds: (1) at what creator count does Palm hire a dedicated client-success/account-manager role; (2) document the chatter-to-creator ratio Palm's external team operates at; (3) use this as input for the in-house chat team roadmap (§8). A 1:10–20 creator-to-account-manager ratio is a concrete benchmark to adopt.

new_findings.topic: Virality pattern recognition as a learnable skill
new_findings.title: Train agency operators to diagnose WHY specific video moments drive views/comments

variants.claim: Learning to identify the precise moment in a video responsible for views or comments — not just copying formats — is the analytical skill that scales organic revenue past $10K/mo.
variants.what_they_do: Griffin reviews videos with operators on calls and annotates specific timestamps: 'this point in the video is why it got views; this point is why it got comments.' Over 4–6 months of repeated exposure, operators internalize the pattern and can evaluate new content without needing external input. It is described as time + experience compressed through deliberate coaching.

sources.quote: every single one of our calls I would try and underline exactly what made a video go viral like why some videos worked why some didn't
you can look at a video and be like this point in the video is why I got so many views or this point in the video is why I got so many comments

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's pipeline (§1) uses AI vision analysis to score viral potential (step 3: transcript, visual breakdown, hook, viral score). This is automated signal — but the human analytical skill of diagnosing WHY a specific moment works is not documented as a training output for Palm staff. Palm has the data layer; the interpretive training layer is a gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a lightweight 'virality annotation' training protocol for Palm editors and content managers: when reviewing top-performing reels, require annotators to tag the exact frame/moment that drove the metric and the mechanism (hook, pattern interrupt, comment bait, etc.). Accumulate these as a labeled training set both for future AI scoring refinement and for human operator skill-building.

new_findings.topic: Traffic continuity as model retention lever
new_findings.title: Treat consistent traffic delivery as the #1 model retention mechanism

variants.claim: Agencies that maintain traffic through IG downturns retain their creators; agencies that can't keep traffic flowing lose models and revenue simultaneously.
variants.what_they_do: This agency explicitly frames multi-channel traffic infrastructure (Meta ads + phone farm) not just as a growth tool but as a creator relationship tool. When IG accounts get restricted, creators see revenue drop and blame the agency. By having non-IG traffic running continuously, the agency absorbs IG volatility without it becoming a creator-facing crisis. The result: agency kept all models during a period when competitors lost creators, and received referrals from satisfied models.

sources.quote: Models are leaving, your revenue is going down... we've been able to keep all of our models, even get referrals, whilst most agencies are losing their creators
you should be providing them traffic even when things are bad

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented creator retention strategy (§9 🔲 GAP) and no multi-channel traffic system (§5 🔲 GAP). If Palm's real creators are currently IG-dependent for traffic, they face the same churn risk described here. The connection between traffic reliability and model retention is not captured in Palm's current framework.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add 'traffic continuity during IG downtime' as an explicit SLA in creator agreements and as a tracked KPI in the whale-hunting / fan-tracker dashboards (§9). Build the narrative internally: Palm's job is to deliver traffic regardless of platform conditions. Use this framing in creator acquisition pitches (§6) as a differentiator vs. single-channel agencies.

new_findings.title: Ad creative is a volume game (1-5 ads per 1k spend)

variants.claim: Acquisition creative is a volume game where consistent high-output testing of hooks, not single perfect ads, finds the winners.
