variants.what_they_do: Targets 1-5 ads per 1k monthly spend, 5-8 new ads/week, with roughly 50 hooks to 5 ad bodies to 2 CTAs; winners are then 'kaleidoscoped' (swap actor, background, speed, captions). Most agencies under-advertise and quit after one failed attempt.

sources.quote: you ideally want to be aiming for one to five ads per 1k in monthly spend
if you're going to be posting like five to 10 ads a week, like you need like 50 hooks

palm_comparison.vs_us: No paid acquisition program at Palm (§6). Relevant later, but the volume-testing principle also maps to Palm's social content engine (§1).
palm_comparison.recommendation: When Palm runs paid acquisition, commit to a fixed weekly ad/hook output cadence and a hook-testing ratio rather than chasing one perfect ad; reuse the inspo pipeline's volume discipline.

new_findings.title: A human brand is the moat against AI commoditization

variants.claim: As AI commoditizes systems and content, an authentic human/founder brand is the durable competitive moat that cannot be replicated.
variants.what_they_do: Frames brand as the only real protection against AI; warns that AI-script 'slop' destroys likeness, and that relatability and authentic personality are what convert attention into trust as faceless/AI content proliferates.

sources.quote: it's the only mode that's going to protect you from AI
AI slop everywhere that does not help your likeness

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm is heavily systems- and AI-leaning (data-driven content §1, AI personas §3) — strengths, but this finding flags that the agency-facing brand layer is an unhedged AI risk (§6/§10).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Pair Palm's AI/systems strengths with a deliberate human brand layer (founder voice) so the agency itself is defensible as AI floods the space.

new_findings.title: Match deal structure to creator tier, not one-size 50/50

variants.claim: Deal structure should flex to the creator's revenue tier instead of applying a single 50/50 split across all creators.
variants.what_they_do: Uses tiered economics: beginners via a paid coaching/productized model (not 50/50), mid-tier (10-50k/mo) on 50/50 plus an upfront setup fee, and whales (100k+/mo) on a lower rev-share plus a fixed monthly retainer (e.g. 20k/mo retainer + 50% of upside above current revenue) so the agency is profitable from month one.

sources.quote: you have to have tiers... Your deal structure has to flex to the tier
I'm going to charge you 20K a month retainer... I only want 50% of what we make you above that 100K

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm sets a single per-creator commission % at onboarding with no documented tier/retainer/upfront model (§7 THIN GAP — the #1 unwritten gap).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a tiered Palm pricing model (upfront setup fee for ad-acquired creators; retainer + upside split for established creators) instead of a flat commission, to protect margins and de-risk acquisition spend.

new_findings.title: Charge no upfront or hidden fees; earn only on a partnership split

variants.claim: Legitimate agencies take no money upfront and no hidden fees, charging only via the revenue split, which is itself a trust differentiator at signing.
variants.what_they_do: Takes nothing upfront and no hidden fees, contrasting against agencies that make creators 'buy their way in'; frames the relationship as partners not assets and lays all terms out from the beginning so they never change.

sources.quote: nothing up front... I've been approached by a lot of other agencies and they all wanted me to buy my way into the agency
we really strive on being partners, you know, and not just treating you like an asset

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm produces a contract PDF at onboarding but has no documented fee-structure stance (§7 GAP); this is a concrete trust-positioning rule to adopt.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Codify 'no upfront, no hidden fees, partner-not-asset' as explicit Palm acquisition trust positioning.

new_findings.title: Take a week off at $100k/month to expose founder dependency before it becomes a ceiling

variants.claim: The only reliable way to test whether a $100k/month agency is truly scalable is for the founder to take a full week off and observe whether revenue, creator satisfaction, and employee efficiency hold without them.
variants.what_they_do: Liam prescribes a literal one-week founder absence as a diagnostic: if GMV and creator satisfaction hold or improve, the business is scalable; if revenue drops or creator issues go unhandled, the business is still founder-dependent and specific SOPs are missing.

sources.quote: take a week off and just observe what happens

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm is early-stage with Josh leading acquisition and strategy hands-on (§6). No documented test for founder dependency exists. Given the current scale, this is a forward-looking tool, but the diagnostic framework is immediately useful to plan against.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Schedule a planned 5-day founder-absence drill (even a partial one — Josh steps back from one department at a time) to surface which processes break. Use the results to prioritize which workflows need SOPs or a designated owner first. Document the gaps in Airtable.

new_findings.topic: VA-staffed repurposing operation — scaling social output without creator time
new_findings.title: Staff a separate VA team to run repurposing accounts, freeing creators for original filming

variants.claim: Repurposing accounts are an operational task fully separable from the creator, enabling unlimited horizontal account scaling via VAs without increasing creator workload.
variants.what_they_do: Once repurposing accounts are set up (distinct identities, rotation rules), the agency hires VAs whose sole job is to download, structurally re-edit, and post content to the repurposing account cluster. The creator only films for the organic primary accounts. This decouples content volume from creator availability.

sources.quote: you can still have your main accounts, your main organic accounts, pull all the views, but you can hire 50 VAs and repost on as many accounts as you like on this side

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm currently uses Amin (contractor) for manual posting (§5) and editors for production (§1). There is no VA layer for repurposing account management. Hiring/delegation infrastructure for this role does not exist yet (§10 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: When Palm is ready to scale social distribution, define a 'Repurposing VA' role with a clear SOP: download source, apply structural edits per checklist, assign to rotation slot, schedule via Publer. Start with one VA per 5 repurposing accounts. Document the SOP before hiring.

new_findings.title: Never mention OnlyFans by name on social — use clean indirect CTAs

variants.claim: Directly referencing OnlyFans on social media platforms (IG, TikTok) risks shadowbans or account termination, so all CTAs must be indirect and discreet.
variants.what_they_do: Instructs operators to keep the social-to-OF funnel link-based but never platform-name the destination in captions, bios phrasing, or spoken content. No five-step click chains either — the funnel is: social content → link → OF page, with the OF brand name omitted from the social layer.

sources.quote: do not mention only fans directly on social media because you are going to get banned. Shadowbanned or worse, right? Just don't do it.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content pipeline (§1) produces social posts but has no documented compliance rules around OF mention avoidance or shadowban risk on IG/TikTok. This is a platform-risk gap (§ platform-risk) that could silently harm creator accounts.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a compliance rule to Palm's content/posting SOP: no direct OF platform name mentions in captions, audio, or on-screen text on IG or TikTok. Standardize CTA language (e.g., 'link in bio,' 'exclusive content,' 'join my page') across all creator accounts. Include this as a checklist item in the editor approval workflow.

new_findings.title: Activate Twitter/X early as a high-virality explicit-content funnel

variants.claim: Twitter allows near-explicit content, making it a materially higher-virality channel for OF funnels than IG/TikTok — agencies should set it up at onboarding and teach creators to reply to viral tweets.
variants.what_they_do: Agency sets up Twitter for any creator who lacks it during onboarding, teaches creator to post more explicit (but compliant) content and to reply to trending/viral tweets to capture organic reach. Frames Twitter as a separate traffic channel from IG/TikTok, not a duplicate.

sources.quote: on Twitter you can get away with more not safeer workor content so AKA showing everything and the chance of that going viral is a lot higher and Twitter's full of that right

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's distribution stack is documented around IG/TikTok via Publer and Amin (§5), with Reddit mentioned as a gap but no Twitter/X SOP. Twitter is absent from the documented pipeline. This is a notable traffic gap (§5 🔲 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add Twitter/X as a standard platform in the social setup checklist for new real-creator onboarding. Build a separate posting cadence for Twitter that allows more explicit previews (within ToS). This is likely a quick win — low setup cost, distinct audience from IG/TikTok, and higher conversion intent.

new_findings.topic: Reddit subreddit and content testing cadence
new_findings.title: Test subreddits by upvote threshold; swap non-performers; prioritise content quality above all

variants.claim: Content quality is the single most important Reddit variable — good content can overcome mediocre subreddits, but bad content fails even in perfect subreddits.
variants.what_they_do: Start with a niche (e.g. feet), find subreddits by searching and crawling competitor model pages. Post in each; if a subreddit doesn't reach ~20+ upvotes, swap it for a new one. Simultaneously test content by finding top-performing posts within target subreddits and asking the creator to recreate them with a personal twist. Scale to 20 subreddits/account then plateau for a month before expanding further.

sources.quote: we basically test the subreddit… if it doesn't get like 20 plus uploads a day then we just change them out for new ones

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content ideation engine (§1) is strong for social reels but is not applied to Reddit content selection. No subreddit testing framework exists (§5 🔲 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adapt Palm's existing inspo-scrape pipeline (§1) to pull top Reddit posts from target subreddits as content briefs — same viral-score logic, different surface. Add subreddit performance tracking (upvote rate per post) to Airtable so low-performing subs are rotated out systematically.

new_findings.title: Launch with a professional booking-call website before signing first creator

variants.claim: Building a credible website with a book-a-call feature on a tool like Lovable before acquiring any creators establishes legitimacy that accelerates creator trust and signing.
variants.what_they_do: Hunter recommends using Lovable (no-code web builder) to create a professional agency site with booking functionality as step one, before any creator outreach. The site signals establishment and reduces friction in creator pitches. He also recommends scanning OFM channels for chatting agencies with proven results to identify outsourcing partners immediately.

sources.quote: I would using lovable create a website with a book a call feature and make it look established and professional.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has an internal admin portal (§0) but no documented public-facing creator acquisition website or booking funnel. Creator onboarding starts with a form wizard after a deal is agreed (§6) — no self-serve discovery or booking flow exists for inbound creator leads.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a public Palm agency site (separate from the admin portal) with: agency positioning, creator success metrics/social proof, and a Calendly or similar book-a-call flow. This supports inbound acquisition from the lead-getter referral program (see acquisition finding above) and paid ads. Low cost to implement given Palm's existing tech capacity.

new_findings.topic: AI model IG account inspiration sourcing
new_findings.title: 1-to-3 ratio filter: only clone IG accounts whose views outpace follower count

variants.claim: Copying an Instagram model whose recent reels have a views-to-followers ratio of at least 1:3 (views ≥ 3× followers) ensures you're piggybacking on a genuinely viral template rather than a stagnant one.
variants.what_they_do: Before cloning or face-swapping any source account, operator audits the last 7 reels and checks that total/average views exceed followers by a factor of 3. Accounts with views below follower count are skipped ('negative ratio'). This is the first filter before any content work begins.

sources.quote: what you're looking for is identify someone that's actually pumping on Instagram so you can benefit from it… ratio of one to three

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content pipeline (§1) scrapes inspo reels and scores them with AI vision (viral score), but the documented filter is engagement-signal-based, not an explicit views-to-followers ratio gate. The 1:3 ratio is a fast, human-readable pre-filter that could complement or precede Palm's AI scoring step. Palm's pipeline applies to real-creator social content; this tactic is described in an AI-creator context but the ratio logic is platform-agnostic.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a views-to-followers ratio check (≥1:3 on last 7 reels) as a hard filter in the Apify scrape → Inspiration queue step (§1, step 1-2). Surface this metric in the inspo review UI alongside the existing viral score so editors and admins can triage faster. Applicable to both real and AI creator pipelines.

new_findings.title: Source first creator clients from personal network (exes, classmates, hometown contacts)

variants.claim: The easiest first clients come from people who already know, like, and trust you — existing personal connections — rather than cold outreach to strangers.
variants.what_they_do: Advises new agency owners to reach out directly to women they already have a personal relationship with: ex-girlfriends, school/university/college peers, or hometown acquaintances. The rationale is that the trust barrier is already cleared, shortening the signing cycle significantly.

sources.quote: reach out to girls who you already know maybe your exes maybe you went to school University College with them whatever maybe you just know somebody from your hometown reach out to those girls those will be your best clients to start off with because they already know like and trust you

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is currently Josh-DM-led outbound to strangers (§6). There is no documented warm-network step in the acquisition funnel. This is a low-cost, zero-infrastructure tactic that Palm could activate immediately for new hires or for bootstrapping a new creator slot.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'warm network sweep' as Step 0 in the creator acquisition playbook (§6): before any cold outreach, new agency operators (or Palm's acquisition lead) should audit personal contacts for women who are already creating or might be interested. Document this as the fastest path to a first signed creator. Combine with a simple referral incentive for signed creators who refer friends.

new_findings.title: Motion control lets you copy trends in minutes

variants.claim: Generating AI versions of trending videos via motion control lets an agency post a trend within minutes instead of waiting days for a creator to film.
variants.what_they_do: Downloads a trending reference video, feeds it plus the AI model image into Cling motion control, and produces the creator's version of the trend in ~5-10 minutes to ride the wave while it is still hot.

sources.quote: see a trend, generate it

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's inspo pipeline (§1) identifies WHAT to film from viral data but still depends on the creator filming. Motion control collapses the produce-time gap Palm currently has.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a motion-control fast-path to the inspo pipeline so promoted trend reels can be AI-rendered for hybrid creators within the trend window.

new_findings.title: AI scores chat transcripts daily

variants.claim: An AI model trained on past chats can read every chatter's transcripts daily and surface buy-signals, missed offers, and coaching feedback that human supervisors cannot cover at scale.
variants.what_they_do: Hive Media pulls Infloww message-board exports into an LLM trained on two years of their own chats to produce daily per-chatter reports (interest/buy signals vs offers sent, left-on-read counts, missed opportunities, KPIs vs peers) because even five supervisors cannot watch 30-40 chatters live.

sources.quote: an AI LLM model that we train... based on two years worth of data of our own chats... how many interest signals... opportunities they missed

palm_comparison.vs_us: Chatting is outsourced and Palm owns no QA loop (§8 OUTSOURCED), but AI chatting is an active Palm project. Direct build-toward for that roadmap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: For Palm's AI-chatting project, design an AI transcript-QA layer that scores every conversation daily (buy-signal capture, offers sent, misses) and feeds coaching back to chatters.

consensus_risers.title: Tag every fan by relationship type and price accordingly

variants.claim: High-converting chat operations segment every subscriber into named relationship/spender tiers and differentiate content, pricing and chatter effort by tier.
variants.what_they_do: Four fan types (transactional, lover, whale, time-waster) marked with emoji labels in Fanvue fan-notes and placed into platform lists; mass-PPV sent per list excluding whales; same content sold at different prices by tier; whales sold to 1:1 at high anchors, never via mass message.

variants.claim: Adding emoji tags (whale emoji, feet emoji, etc.) directly to a fan's OnlyFans display name lets chatters instantly identify spend tier and kink preference without opening notes, critical when managing high inbox volume.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters edit the fan's name field in the OF inbox to prepend relevant emojis: 🐳 for whale, specific kink emojis (e.g., foot emoji for feet fetish). This means the chatter sees the tag the moment the chat appears in the list and can tailor the opening message without reading back through history.

variants.claim: Low/non-spenders need fast, transactional, no-brainer openings, while high spenders need slower, personalized, emotionally invested approaches that reflect the higher ROI of putting time into them.
variants.what_they_do: Two distinct opening playbooks: (1) Low/non-spenders (<$50 max purchase): 'Two choices' illusion (free thing + heavily discounted PPV framed as a favor); 'Question game' (ask 2–3 preference questions about color/toy/angle, disappear briefly, return with a PPV 'chosen based on their answers'). Goal is just to get the first purchase and enter the loop. (2) High spenders (>$120 max purchase): 'Opinion opening' (frame a PPV as something the creator is nervous/unsure about, ask for their trusted opinion — if they object to price, say you're 'impartial' and it makes them more curious); 'Personalized PPV' for lapsed high spenders (manually send a high-quality video with a caption referencing their past purchases, their interests from fan notes, priced $150–200, with fan's name in the first 5 words of the last message sent).

sources.quote: Mark all spenders on relationship types
Target each group with specific mass messages
you can put emojis in their name, right? In their OnlyFans name and you can quickly see what type of subscriber they are
a specific kink they might have if they're into feet. You can put a feet emoji in their name
if we were talking to, say, a low or non-spender, we want to be able to transition in a way that is fast and efficient and make that first pay-per-view seem like a no-brainer
it's pretty much exclusively for non-active high spenders. So people that used to spend a lot of money, but don't really talk anymore

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has Fan Tracker/whale-hunting data collection (§9 PARTIAL) but chatting is outsourced (§8) with no in-house fan-tiering SOP or differential-pricing model; pricing is also a gap (§7).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a fan-tier taxonomy plus differential-PPV pricing ladder into the in-house/AI chatting roadmap, fed from existing Fan Tracker data now; pricing applies to real creators today.

consensus_risers.title: Replace yourself via ownership ladder, not task delegation

variants.claim: Founders scale out of the business by delegating outcomes and decision-making (an ownership ladder), not by dumping tasks, which only deepens dependency.
variants.what_they_do: Audits dollar-value-per-hour (only hires when a low-DVPH task blocks a high one), develops people up a level 1-5 accountability dial from doer to owner, and installs a decision filter so nobody escalates a problem without bullets + 2-3 solutions + a recommendation; uses the '30-day disappear test' to find the ceiling.

variants.claim: Scale past a plateau by training department heads to own problems end-to-end rather than escalating every decision to the owner.
variants.what_they_do: Five accountability levels: (1) wait to be told, (2) bring the problem, (3) bring problem + 1-2 solutions, (4) solve it and report, (5) solve it AND build an SOP so it never recurs. Most teams sit at 1-2, making the owner the bottleneck. Push C-suite heads to level 4-5; example: an acquisition manager independently bought aged warmed IG accounts and hired another VA to lift outreach capacity, then just reported it. Hold Mon-Fri morning team calls with only the department heads.

variants.claim: Heavy supervision layers and clear workflows let the founder step away while the business keeps running, which is the real test of a scaled agency.
variants.what_they_do: Hive Media (300 employees, 60 models) layers supervisors into category managers into shift/market managers per department, and the founder says if he disappeared for six months the company would still run profitably.

sources.quote: delegation actually works is if you delegate outcomes plus decision-making instead of delegating tasks
never bring me a problem without what happened... 2-3 solutions, your recommendation
there are five main levels of accountability... the first level being they just simply wait for the command on what to do.
what's different between level five and level four is they not only find the problem and solve it, but they also will build infrastructure to ensure that this problem never happens again.
making sure that you guys do morning team calls Monday to Friday.
if I disappear for 6 months right now, will my company grow? Maybe not exponentially, but it will run

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's org is thin (§10 GAP): no org chart, ownership model, or decision framework; founder/tech concentrated on Josh and Evan.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt an ownership-ladder + decision-filter operating model so Palm functions (acquisition, content, chat, ops) each have an owner and decisions don't route through the founders — directly feeds the teammate-org initiative.

consensus_risers.topic: Free teaser content as pre-sell warm-up
consensus_risers.title: Free teaser → $5–15 qualifier → $30–40+ escalation PPV ladder

variants.claim: Sending free 'outfit' or soft-tease images before the first paid offer raises arousal and reduces friction on the first purchase, making the entire escalation ladder more likely to convert.
variants.what_they_do: At the start of each scripted session, the chatter sends 1–2 free images — typically a suggestive but non-explicit 'outfit' shot — with no price attached, framed as casual sharing ('what do you think about this outfit?'). Only after the subscriber reacts positively does the chatter pivot to the paid ladder. This gates the sell behind a micro-engagement that confirms interest and builds investment.

variants.claim: Starting PPV with a free non-revealing image, then a small $5–15 paid image, then escalating to $30–40+ video creates a progressive upsell funnel that qualifies spenders before committing to expensive content.
variants.what_they_do: Chatter sends (1) a free, non-revealing image to open the conversation; (2) a slightly more revealing image/clip priced $5–15 to qualify whether the fan will spend; (3) subsequent escalating PPV pieces priced $30–40+. Welcome/intro message is auto-sent at $15–25 on subscribe. Each step is treated as a 'drink at the bar' — the fan self-selects into a higher spend tier organically.

variants.claim: Sending a blurred or cropped content preview instead of the full asset creates curiosity that converts fans to purchase the unlock.
variants.what_they_do: Rather than describing content verbally or sending it outright, chatters send a teaser (blurred image or cropped clip) accompanied by a curiosity-hook line such as 'guess what's under this honey, unlock it to find out.' The preview functions as a visual purchase prompt.

sources.quote: I'll maybe send you a couple pictures just for free no price attached of my outfit maybe I look cute I'm like yeah what do you think about this outfit
ideally it's something sexual that makes the subscriber horny and he's like okay yeah that's hot... now after that you can be like yo do you want me to show a bit more
the next video or picture will be a little more revealing you'll sell that for five to ten dollars and so on and so forth the next one from there will be 30 to 40
the first ppv messages should be pictures that are like five to ten dollars you're just trying to qualify if this guy is a spender or a Time waster
use teasers and previews instead of sending the full content immediately send a blurred or cropped preview to spark curiosity

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal chat SOP exists at Palm (§8). This specific 'free teaser before first pitch' mechanic is a discrete, concrete tactic to encode into the future chatter SOP and AI chatting state machine.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a mandatory 'warm-up phase' in the chat SOP: 1 free teaser image sent before any PPV offer, followed by a reaction-check message. Only proceed to the $15 entry PPV after a positive subscriber response. This applies directly to AI chatting — build the state machine so it cannot enter 'sell mode' before completing the warm-up phase.

consensus_risers.title: Log personal fan details in notes so any chatter can continue the relationship seamlessly

variants.claim: Chatters add personal details (job, location, interests) to each fan's notes or rename field so any chatter who picks up the conversation later can maintain the illusion of a continuous, invested relationship.
variants.what_they_do: After a fan shares personal information in DMs, the chatter immediately records it in that fan's notes inside the OF messaging interface (or an external CRM). The fan's name field may be renamed to include key tags. When a different chatter takes over a shift, they review notes first and reference those personal details naturally — the fan feels remembered and valued, which increases LTV.

variants.claim: Chatters who record personal details shared by fans during conversations can use those details in future sessions to create a sense of genuine ongoing relationship.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are instructed to jot down fan-disclosed details — name, age, relationship status, hobbies, personal anecdotes — and reference them in later messages. The explicit goal is to make the fan feel 'seen, heard, and valued' rather than like one of many subscribers.

variants.claim: Building rapport with subscribers requires learning what they are genuinely into (fetishes, hobbies, job) and recording those details in the subscriber's notes field to enable personalized follow-up.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters skip surface-level questions (age, location) and instead probe for fetishes, hobbies, and occupation. Every paying subscriber gets a notes entry with those details so any chatter picking up the conversation later has context. Non-spenders are deprioritized — no time is wasted building rapport with subs who have not spent money.

sources.quote: we'll have our chatting team add notes to each fan where you can write down personable information and the next chatter that hops on to talk to that dude will kind of have an idea
maybe he's told the girl what he does for a living where he lives and we'll put all that information in his notes or in his name
jot down details about them you want to know more than just their name keep track of things like their age relationship status and any personal tibbits they share
what are they into right maybe they what do they do for work what are their Hobbies obviously if we're talking about only fans specifically what are they into like what are their fetishes
you don't want to be able to report with people who are just going to waste your time

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a Fan Tracker CRM and whale-hunting tools (§9), but no written retention/chatter handoff SOP. The notes-per-fan mechanic described here is a direct operational input for the Fan Tracker and for the in-house chat team SOP.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Formalise a 'fan card' field set in the Fan Tracker (or OF notes) — at minimum: spending tier, personal details shared, last PPV purchased, last contact date. Require the external chat team to populate this today; make it mandatory in the in-house chat SOP. Aligns with and extends §9 whale-hunting data.

consensus_risers.topic: signing call vibe and red flags
consensus_risers.title: Treat the signing call as a two-way interview with hard non-negotiables

variants.claim: Operators sign more durable creators by qualifying them on the call against work-ethic and commitment non-negotiables instead of signing whoever replies.
variants.what_they_do: Opens the call human-first (camera on, 'how are you', hobbies) to break barriers, then qualifies: rejects short-term-money motives and 'overly involved' creators, requires committed daily hours and a fixed content/live cadence, and walks away if the vibe or non-negotiables fail.

variants.claim: Agencies should qualify creators on mindset — specifically filtering out those who want purely passive income — because they disengage during the experimentation phase and waste agency resources.
variants.what_they_do: The speakers describe two acquisition filters: (1) prefer creators who have already tried OF themselves and seen some traction, because they understand the work involved and appreciate agency value more; (2) screen out creators who articulate 'I just want to make money passively' as their primary motivation, since they won't fulfill content obligations and the agency's marketing investment becomes a gamble. They also describe a 7-step creator vetting process for creators evaluating agencies (social proof → documented process → call → ask deliverable questions → watch for red flags like guaranteed numbers or pressure to sign quickly), which implicitly describes what a credible agency's pitch should contain.

variants.claim: How a model responds during onboarding — responsiveness, receptiveness to feedback, implementation of early requests — is a leading indicator of her long-term revenue potential and whether the agency should continue investing in her.
variants.what_they_do: They observe model behavior during the onboarding phase as a quality signal: slow responses, failure to implement feedback, or resistance to criticism are treated as early warnings that the creator will not reach top-tier earnings. They acknowledge that new agencies must work with whoever they can get but advise building judgment over time to screen for quality clients.

sources.quote: work ethic trumps everything
have non-negotiables and have some respect for yourself
we prefer it's between 5 and six because then that allows a lot more content to be made

me personally when it comes to new creators I definitely would say I'm kind of looking at the mindset that they're in before coming into this like if they're just in this I just want to make money passively then forget it it's not going to work
if they're flaky or they don't respond very good or they take forever to respond or they don't Implement criticism you most likely don't have a very high quality client

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm acquisition is Josh-DM-led with no documented signing-call qualification framework (§6 THIN); this is a build-toward script Palm lacks.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt a documented signing-call SOP: human-first rapport, explicit work-ethic/cadence non-negotiables, and willingness to reject misaligned creators.

consensus_risers.title: Target mid-tier creators (10K followers, ~$1-3K/mo) not top earners

variants.claim: Beginner agencies should target creators with ~10-15K followers already making $1-3K/month rather than chasing large verified accounts who won't respond.
variants.what_they_do: Explicitly filter outreach to creators in the 10K-15K follower range earning roughly $1-3K/month on OF, on the basis that they are more open to support, more reachable via DM, and more likely to convert to a managed relationship.

variants.claim: The highest-converting creator prospects are those who are stuck at a plateau, overwhelmed by workload, or have had a bad agency experience — not cold beginners.
variants.what_they_do: Agency explicitly looks for creators who: (a) plateaued after initial growth, (b) are overwhelmed managing content + chatting alone, (c) had a bad prior agency and are open to a better partner. Uses these pain points as the outreach hook.

variants.claim: Agencies should segment potential creator clients into four revenue bands and target the tier where their service delivers the highest ROI.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan segments OF creators into four bands: $0–10K/mo, $10–50K/mo, $100–500K/mo, and $500K+/mo. New/smaller agencies are directed to target the $0–10K band because those creators get the biggest ROI from management services. He sends clients a segmentation sheet and has them identify which band to focus on exclusively, so outreach and pitching is concentrated rather than scattered.

sources.quote: targeting more beginner friendly creators as well. Also, someone that's kind of starting out, 10,000 15,000 followers, ones that are also making around 10K a month or so
you're looking for models that are a bit overwhelmed, they're a bit stuck, their accounts are being plateaued, maybe they've had a bad agency before

we split there up into four market segments right only fans models making 500k a month only fans models making between 100 500k a month only fans models making 10 to 50K a month and only fans models anything from 0 to 10K

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is Josh-DM-led with no documented targeting criteria (§6 GAP). No internal baseline exists for follower count, current earnings, or engagement thresholds that qualify a creator for outreach.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Codify a minimum viable creator profile for outreach: e.g., 10K-20K IG followers, $1-3K/mo existing OF revenue, active posting cadence. Add these as qualification fields to the creator onboarding form or a pre-onboarding screening step in Airtable.

consensus_risers.topic: Objection handling — add value before discounting
consensus_risers.title: When a subscriber objects to price, add more content first; only discount by removing content, not by lowering the price

variants.claim: Reflexively discounting when a subscriber says 'too expensive' trains them to always haggle; instead, offer more content at the same price first, and if a discount is unavoidable, remove content from the bundle rather than simply lowering the stated price.
variants.what_they_do: Rule 1 — Value-add first: if a sub resists price, add a small extra (bonus clip, picture set) and make it a huge deal, framing it as a personal favor. This gets full price paid and doesn't establish a discount expectation. Rule 2 — Renegotiation discount: if discounting is unavoidable, pre-load the PPV with a few extra pictures/clips by default. When the sub asks for less, remove those extras and drop the price — so the price reduction is tied to a concrete content reduction, not arbitrary. This preserves the perception that prices are meaningful. Rule 3 — Underpriced sub recovery: if a sub accepts a dropped price too readily, immediately propose a larger bundle ('I had an idea — I want to throw in extra stuff') to recapture upside.

variants.claim: When a fan objects to a custom content price, offering 50% upfront and 50% on delivery preserves the full price while reducing the perceived financial risk — avoiding the margin hit of a discount.
variants.what_they_do: Chatter expresses genuine excitement about the custom idea, adds her own suggestions to raise perceived effort/value, then quotes a premium price. On price objection: offer to deliver 'today/in 30 minutes' (urgency) and propose 50% now, 50% after delivery. Only drop the price as a final fallback if the 50/50 offer also fails.

sources.quote: it is a better first response to try and give them something small to sweeten the deal, and you get paid the full amount as opposed to just discounting
if they ask for a discount, you can take that stuff out and then drop the price, so you've now renegotiated
you can offer him 50/50. So he pays 50% before and 50% after
instead of dropping the price, you can tell him to pay half now, pay half later

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal objection-handling framework exists at Palm (§8 outsourced). This is build-toward intelligence for both the in-house chat team and the AI chatting project, where objection-handling logic needs to be systematized.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Make 'add value before discounting, renegotiate via content removal' a non-negotiable chatter policy in the future in-house SOP. For the AI chatting project, encode this as a decision tree: objection detected → attempt value-add response → if second objection → remove content and lower price. Also document the underpriced-sub recovery move as a standard upsell trigger.

consensus_risers.topic: Notion-based fulfillment OS with per-role task board views
consensus_risers.title: Build role-filtered Notion task boards so each team member sees only their queue

variants.claim: A single Notion database for all fulfillment tasks, filtered into separate board views per role (videographer, editor, social media manager, CSM, strategist), eliminates cross-team confusion and missed deadlines without requiring multiple databases.
variants.what_they_do: One 'actions' Notion database with status stages (ready to script → scripting → ready to shoot → ready to edit → editing → ready to review → ready to send to client → sent to client → client approved → ready to post → done). Project templates pre-populate task lists (e.g., 8 videos + 3 ads for flagship offer). Each role gets a filtered view showing only their relevant statuses: videographer sees shoot-date calendar; editor sees editing dates and a countdown timer; CSM sees 'ready for review' and 'ready to send to client'; social media manager sees post-date calendar. Internal comments replace Slack/iMessage for revision feedback.

variants.claim: A scaled agency runs on connected central databases (accounts, team, content, finance) so operational detail lives in systems, not the owner's head.
variants.what_they_do: Runs six linked Notion systems (model account manager with logins/warmup SOPs, VA/team tracker with pay+KPIs, content HQ, finance tracker, iPhone tracker, AI to-do list) that interlink so the owner only reviews and decides.

sources.quote: we're going to assign owners at each stages so that it's really really clear
we created a board for that. So, we're going to go to video editor board and boom, ready to edit
the comment. I'm not communicating via Slack. I'm not communicating via iMessage. It's right here.
the model account manager

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a mature 8-step content pipeline (§1) with Assets and Tasks tables in Airtable, an editor workflow with day-counter task queue, and 2-slot-per-day scheduling. This is structurally similar. Key gap: Palm's documented editor workflow covers submission→library→grid planner but does not describe per-role filtered views or cross-role handoff statuses within a single task record. The Notion approach adds a unified card that travels through all roles, keeping script, shoot footage, and edit all on one record.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Evaluate whether Palm's Airtable Tasks table can be extended with status stages and per-role filtered views (editor view, social media manager view, creator view) rather than rebuilding in Notion. The specific add: attach raw footage links, captions, and revision comments directly to the task card so editors and Amin don't need Slack/Telegram for task-level communication. This is a concrete improvement to the existing mature system.

consensus_risers.title: Do every role yourself first, document it, then hand it off

variants.claim: Learn each part of the business personally and turn it into an SOP before delegating, so hires run a documented process.
variants.what_they_do: Owner did outreach/closing/onboarding/marketing/chatting himself first, then wrote an SOP doc + Loom video for each task so new hires self-train from the document instead of hours of live training. Hires for hunger/reliability/coachability over experience (experienced people resist the system). Tracks KPIs on every function (DMs sent, replies, calls booked, clients closed, revenue per chatter).

variants.claim: Founders should reallocate large blocks of daily time to the single highest-leverage activity (brand/distribution) and treat inability to do so as a sign the business is structured wrong.
variants.what_they_do: Argues for 4+ hours/day on personal brand and says if you don't have that time, the business is set up wrong and you're stuck firefighting; pushes decentralizing operations so the founder works on leverage, not in the hamster wheel.

sources.quote: you funnel all of that traffic over to Only Fans. And then the second thing is the chatting.
One of the first things that you need to do is connect their Only Fans account to your Inflow.
you need to be spending 4 hours a day on this. Like minimum
if you haven't got 4 hours... a day to put into your personal brand, you're doing something wrong with your business

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm is strong on productized content tooling (§1) but has no stated document-before-delegate discipline; team/org THIN (§10).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Standardize SOP + Loom documentation per role before backfilling it, hire for coachability, and attach KPIs to each function.

consensus_risers.title: Configure an auto-sent paid intro message ($15–25) to fire on every new subscriber

variants.claim: Setting up an automated PPV intro message that triggers the moment a fan subscribes generates passive revenue from every new sub with zero chatter effort.
variants.what_they_do: Inside OF settings, a welcome message is pre-loaded with a PPV attachment priced at $15–25. It fires automatically to every new subscriber. No chatter action required. This is the first monetization touchpoint before any live chat begins, and it also flags whether the new sub is a likely spender (purchase = qualified lead for the upsell ladder).

variants.claim: An automated message sent to every new subscriber that creates a conversational scenario and pitches a first video purchase converts cold new fans into paying customers before a live chatter has to engage.
variants.what_they_do: A pre-written automated script goes to every new sub immediately after they subscribe. It creates a light interactive scenario (not specified in detail), leads the fan through a short interaction, and closes with a $34.99 video offer. Once the fan buys, they are flagged as 'warmed up' and handed to regular chat flow at full price floors.

sources.quote: another way that you can quickly gauge how interested a fan is and your content is if they purchase your intro message this is a message that you set up to automatically send out once they hit subscribe
usually our welcome messages are like 15 to 25 bucks but then the first ppv we send is like 5 to 15
it will send to the sub it will create a little scenario it will get them to do something and then it will sell them a video for $34.99

palm_comparison.vs_us: No Palm-documented equivalent (§8 outsourced, §7 GAP on PPV setup). This is a zero-marginal-effort revenue lever that the external chat team can implement immediately for every Palm creator, and should be baked into the in-house chat onboarding checklist.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Immediately brief the external chat team to verify every Palm creator has an auto-send intro PPV configured at $15–25. Make this a checklist item in creator onboarding (§6 form wizard). Track intro-PPV purchase rate per creator as a leading indicator of subscriber quality.

consensus_risers.title: Run 3-variant script tests with unlock-rate tracking before standardising any chatting SOP

variants.claim: Running three concurrent script variants with measured unlock rates — rather than just deploying a new script agency-wide — reveals the specific element driving performance and prevents agencies from unknowingly using inferior scripts.
variants.what_they_do: When a new chatting script is introduced, the agency assigns variant A, B, and C to different chatters or conversation batches, tracks PPV unlock rate per variant, diagnoses why the winning variant beat the others (e.g., opening line framing), and only then canonises the best approach across all chatters.

variants.claim: The most effective chat scripts are built bottom-up from live testing — chatters freestyle, track what gets positive responses and high PPV buy rates, and promote those lines/setups into the official script iteratively.
variants.what_they_do: The agency does not hand chatters a finished script to copy-paste. They provide the structural framework (rapport → passive tease → yes train → PPV ladder → gratification close) and then chatters freestyle within it. When a specific line or PPV setup consistently produces positive fan responses or high buy rates, it gets promoted into the agency's canonical script. The script is therefore a living document refined by actual conversion data.

sources.quote: test every single one of them. Okay, this script got a 50% unlock rate. The other variation is 30%, this is 55. Okay, why?
If we see that a certain sentence has positive feedback from most subscribers, we will use it in the script. If a PPV has a high buy rate with a certain PPV setup, we will use it in the script
how we create scripts in our agency is we get on the page, we start chatting and see what works

palm_comparison.vs_us: Chatting is currently outsourced at Palm (§8 — OUTSOURCED); no internal script management or test infrastructure exists today. This is a build-toward finding for the planned in-house and AI chatting projects.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When designing Palm's in-house chat SOP (§8 roadmap), build variant testing into the process architecture from day one: store script variants in Airtable with a linked 'test result' table capturing unlock rate and revenue per test run. Do not import a competitor's script wholesale — always test 3 variants first.

consensus_risers.title: Daily feedback cadence to accelerate employee ramp 7x vs weekly

variants.claim: Giving employees feedback every single day instead of once a week makes them progress seven times faster, cutting ramp time from seven months to thirty days.
variants.what_they_do: Agency owner or a designated lead delivers individual feedback to every employee daily — not a team standup, but specific corrective or reinforcing input tied to that person's KPIs and tasks. This is paired with formal performance reviews on a recurring schedule.

variants.claim: The fastest way to improve a new chatter is to sit on a live call with them while they chat, give feedback within seconds, and focus on fixing only one thing at a time.
variants.what_they_do: Manager joins a live call with the chatter while they work in real time. Feedback is immediate ('stop, do it again'). Manager identifies the single highest-leverage improvement point and works only on that until fixed, then moves to the next. Chatters are not 'saved' — manager tells them what to do but makes them execute it themselves so the learning sticks.

sources.quote: give your employees feedback once a week, I give them feedback every single day. That means my employees are going to progress seven times faster
Fast feedback loops. If you want someone to learn something quickly, you need there to be a short gap between when they do something and when they get feedback on it. And I mean seconds
Don't step in and do it for them. You can tell them what to do. That's how they actually learn

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented feedback cadence for editors, Amin, or any contractor (§10 — THIN). There are no performance review cycles or KPI dashboards per employee in the codebase. The closest analogue is the editor task queue in the portal, which surfaces output but not a feedback loop.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Establish a lightweight daily async check-in for each active role (editor, posting contractor, eventually chatters): a shared Telegram thread or Airtable comment thread where the reviewer flags one specific 'fix this' and one 'good job' per day per person. Formalise this before hiring the in-house chat team (~1 yr roadmap item, §8).

consensus_risers.topic: Agency value proposition positioning for creator signing
consensus_risers.title: Lead creator pitches with organic social capability, not chat or back-end ops

variants.claim: The value proposition that closes creator deals in 2026 is demonstrable front-end marketing capability — organic social reach and traffic generation — not chatting or business infrastructure.
variants.what_they_do: The consultant advises that agencies pitching creators should lead with their organic social marketing system and track record of growing audiences, since that is what creators cannot do themselves and cannot easily get elsewhere at differentiated quality.

variants.claim: The binding constraint on agency growth is obscurity, not lead supply, so building a known brand is the highest-leverage acquisition asset.
variants.what_they_do: Argues all growth is constrained by irrelevance; a brand compounds trust before the first conversation so a large share of pipeline becomes inbound, and reframes competition as obscurity rather than other agencies. Notes ~7M creators means the market is nowhere near saturated.

sources.quote: the greatest value you can possibly offer a new model is on the front end. Using organic social to create marketing campaigns and distribution networks that generate massive volumes of high quality traffic
your real constraint isn't leads. It's irrelevance. Nobody knows you exist
80% of my pipeline is from inbound, from my brand

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition system is Josh-DM-led with no systematic pitch framework documented (§6 GAP). It is unknown whether current pitches lead with Palm's organic social strength. Given Palm's mature content engine (§1), this capability should be front-and-center in every creator conversation but is not yet formalized.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a formal creator pitch deck or one-pager that leads with Palm's organic social system: data-driven inspo sourcing, AI-assisted analysis, and proven posting infrastructure. Include any growth metrics from existing creators. This directly addresses the §6 GAP in systematic acquisition and gives Josh a repeatable, differentiating pitch rather than ad-hoc DM conversations.

consensus_risers.title: Scrape a 100k-subreddit master list keyed to creator physical attributes, then niche-match posts

variants.claim: Rather than manually searching for relevant subreddits, build a scraped database of 100,000+ subreddits tagged by niche attributes so any new creator can be immediately mapped to every viable subreddit based on her physical characteristics.
variants.what_they_do: Agency built (or uses) a scraper that pulls every subreddit and tags it by niche (ethnicity, body type, fetish, etc.). When a creator is onboarded, her attributes (blonde, big breasts, e-girl, etc.) are matched against the database to generate her posting list. Volume over precision: one piece of content gets posted across every marginally relevant subreddit. Key insight: NSFW content also gets cross-posted into SFW subreddits (makeup, car repair, GeoGuessr) using curiosity/cleavage tactics to reach users who have never enabled NSFW settings.

variants.claim: Beginners should copy the structure and tone of high-performing captions in their target subreddits (not verbatim) and only develop original voice after accumulating posting experience.
variants.what_they_do: Find the most-upvoted posts in the subreddits being targeted. Extract caption patterns (length, phrasing style, call-to-action type). Write similar-but-not-identical captions. After sufficient volume, the operator develops intuition for original captions. Treat caption testing as iterative — same test-and-replace logic applied to subreddits and content.

sources.quote: what we would do is we would just have a scraper that would basically scrape every single subreddit that would or could be tied to either one of these niches. You'd build up a list of subreddits. You'd have a 100,000 subreddits

there's also a lot of say for work subreddits where people would just, you know, I don't know, ask for advice for piercings in their ears or or makeup advice. And you could also post in these subreddits
we take inspiration a lot of the time from bigger models that are doing well in the subre we post in… make it similar but not the exact same

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no Reddit presence (§5 GAP). Palm's content pipeline (§1) does use Apify scraping for IG inspiration, so the scraping capability exists — it would need to be redirected to Reddit subreddit discovery. The SFW infiltration angle is completely absent from any Palm workflow.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Commission a one-time scrape of Reddit subreddits tagged by adult-content niche to build a master lookup table. Store in Airtable alongside creator attribute fields. On creator onboarding, auto-generate a subreddit posting list. Prioritise NSFW subreddits first; layer in SFW curiosity subreddits (lifestyle, hobby) only once the core strategy is proven.

consensus_risers.title: Salary creator model: pay creator fixed rate, agency keeps ~90% of revenue

variants.claim: In a vertical-scale agency, paying creators a fixed monthly salary (e.g. $2,500–$10,000) regardless of page revenue allows the agency to retain ~90% of profits.
variants.what_they_do: Agency signs creators on a flat monthly salary tied to cost-of-living (e.g. $2,500–$10,000/mo depending on location). If the page earns $100K/mo, the agency keeps ~$90K. new_evidence.title: Warm fans with a slightly discounted first PPV, then escalate price on every subsequent send

variants.claim: The first PPV sale is a warm-up purchase at a slight discount; once a fan buys it, every future PPV should be priced at the floor or higher, escalating as the conversation progresses.
variants.what_they_do: New subscribers receive an automated script that pitches a first video at $34.99 (slightly below the $39.99 floor) to reduce friction and get a first purchase. After that purchase the fan is considered 'warmed up' and all future content is priced at $40+ for video and $20+ for images, escalating upward as the sexting session progresses. The escalation is tied to emotional momentum — the longer the conversation runs, the more the fan will pay.

variants.what_they_do: Luca Pritchard caps the first PPV at $5-$20, never higher, to get the customer used to the habit of buying on the platform before laddering up; habibi tests a brand-new buyer with a small first buy (e.g. $9) to read his boundaries.

variants.claim: The first PPV sale should always be $5 or less to condition the subscriber to buy; subsequent sales increase in price incrementally, and the overall set is structured as 7 separate sales attempts at progressively higher price points.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are instructed to structure one content 'set' as 7 distinct sales opportunities (individual pics, pic+video combos, etc.) with prices rising at each step. The first sale is always ≤$5 — not because the content is cheap, but to make the buy frictionless and establish a Pavlovian habit: sub sees PPV → sub unlocks. Subs who won't spend $5 are deprioritized or ignored. New chatters are taught to use small price increments (e.g., $5 → $7 → $10) rather than jumping to $25+. Selling too much too soon is explicitly called out as a sub-burning mistake; longevity and lifetime value are the stated goal.

variants.claim: Starting the PPV ladder at a low price ($15) minimizes the psychological barrier to the first purchase, making it far more likely the subscriber enters the escalation funnel and ultimately spends $300–$500.
variants.what_they_do: The specific ladder cited: $15 → $30 → $60 → $120 → $200 → $200. Entry price is low enough to feel trivial. Each step roughly doubles, creating anchoring where each subsequent price feels proportional to the last rather than shocking. The final $200 items are 'full action' — maximum explicitness justifying maximum price. Total session value: ~$625 at full conversion.

variants.claim: Sending low-ticket PPVs ($15–$20) first gauges a subscriber's willingness to spend and builds purchasing habit before high-ticket ($80–$100+) asks.
variants.what_they_do: Chatter begins by offering content in the $15–$20 range ('dipping a toe in the water'). After multiple purchases at this level, the chatter reads the subscriber's comfort and escalates to high-ticket PPVs ($80, $90, $100+). No high-ticket offer is sent cold to a new subscriber.

variants.claim: A soft verbal commitment ('yes train') before sending the first PPV — combined with a $15–$25 price point and partial-nudity content — significantly increases buy-through rates.
variants.what_they_do: Before sending any PPV, the chatter runs a 'yes train': a question that sounds like consent ('if I do this, do you promise you'll go easy on me?') but functions as a purchase pre-commitment. When the fan says yes, he feels psychologically obligated to buy the PPV that follows. First PPV is priced $15–$25 (higher if the model is positioned as premium; lower if marketed with explicit free content). Content is partial nudity only (top off). Subsequent PPVs escalate: bottoms off → full nude → solo play. Between each PPV, the chatter rebuilds arousal with free teasers and descriptive text before the next ask. The session ends when the fan signals spending fatigue — at which point no further PPV is pushed.

new_evidence.title: Personalized openers using username or profile detail to force engagement

variants.claim: Sending a message personalized to a specific subscriber's username or visible profile detail dramatically increases response rates versus generic openers.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are required to craft a unique opener for each sub that contains a play on the subscriber's username (e.g., 'Hey Jimmy, are you the same Jimmy Johnson from the Dallas Cowboys?'). The opener must include exactly one question — no more — so it opens a single conversational thread the sub feels obligated to answer. Copy-paste openers like 'hey how are you' or 'thanks for the follow' are explicitly banned. The opener is designed to (a) prove a human is typing, (b) put the chatter in conversational control by triggering a reaction, and (c) give the sub an easy, interesting thing to respond to.

variants.claim: The PPV caption is the last decision point before purchase; embedding specific, timestamped curiosity hooks (rather than generic hype) dramatically increases unlock rates by making the sub feel something personalized and unmissable is inside.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters/copywriters craft captions ending with one or more of these proven curiosity hooks: (1) Timestamped surprise — 'I can't believe I did that at 2:41' (specificity signals authenticity); (2) Personalized connection — 'I want to see if you can spot which part I was thinking about you'; (3) Challenge/gamification — 'There's no way you'll last past what I did at minute 1:21'; (4) Embarrassment/vulnerability — 'I'm pretty embarrassed I did that towards the end, but oh well'; (5) Physical detail — 'I'm still sore from that last part'; (6) Multi-content choice — 'Which one of those positions was your favorite?' These are used as the final sentence only; the rest of the caption continues the sexting/emotional build-up from the conversation. Specificity (exact timestamps, specific details) is emphasised as what makes hooks feel genuine vs scripted.

variants.claim: A short, delayed welcome message (3 min after sub) that references the fan's profile bio/name outperforms generic paragraphs because it signals authenticity and avoids setting a low price anchor.
variants.what_they_do: Use Creator Hero's delayed-message feature to send a simple 'Hi' or name-based joke 3 minutes after subscription. If no reply, follow up with a profile-specific observation (name joke, bio reference) to signal a real person is behind the account. Never send a PPV or long paragraph as the first message.

variants.claim: Using open-ended, flirty questions instead of generic greetings like 'hey' or 'how are you' dramatically increases subscriber engagement.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are trained to never open with 'hello' or 'how are you'; instead they ask about the fan's day in a flirty, playful way, reference previous conversations ('you made me blush'), and use the subscriber's name periodically to create intimacy. Example opener: 'Hey handsome I was just thinking about our last conversation you made me blush.'

variants.claim: Personalizing chats with the fan's name and avoiding generic mass-message bundle blasts is a high-leverage, underused driver of spend and LTV.
variants.what_they_do: Habibi stresses using the fan's actual name instead of 'babe' (people respond to their own name) and refuses mass-message bundle blasts and odd-number 'flea market' pricing, taking a new account from $33 to $500+ days with one-to-one personalized chatting.

new_evidence.title: Make the fan chase to hold power

variants.claim: Letting the fan initiate and withholding rather than over-giving keeps the model in the power position and increases buying.
variants.what_they_do: Luca Pritchard plays hot-and-cold and sends non-nude teasers so the customer initiates, putting the chatter in the power position; habibi withholds (tells a fan asking for photos 'no, you're going to have to wait') to drive desire before selling.

variants.claim: Deliberately breaking rapport with playful negative statements (e.g., 'I was starting to like you until you said that') and push-pull phrasing prevents the sub from losing interest and causes them to actively seek the chatter's validation — which is the precondition for purchasing.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are drilled on two borrowed-from-pickup techniques: (1) Rapport breaks — any statement that actively discourages the deepening of connection, always softened as a joke (e.g., 'you and I would never get along because you're a math teacher and I have such trauma about math teachers'). These are not standalone — they must be followed up or contextualized so they don't read as mean over text. (2) Push-pulls — a positive statement immediately followed by a negative, or vice versa (e.g., 'you're actually kind of funny and I bet people teased you for it growing up'). These are to be peppered throughout the entire conversation, not saved for one moment. The psychological goal: sub chases chatter's validation, which increases desire to please — i.e., spend money.

variants.claim: Chatters should never directly initiate sexual conversation — instead, send an ambiguous teaser that makes sexual escalation the fan's idea, reducing friction and preserving the illusion of authenticity.
variants.what_they_do: Chatter sends a low-reveal teaser (e.g., model in a towel with wet hair, caption: 'just got out of the shower') with no sexual framing. The fan reads the signal and responds sexually — he has technically initiated. Alternatively, a 3-second clip of the model waving to camera with a self-deprecating caption ('my face looks so bad right now') achieves the same passive initiation. Once the fan escalates, the chatter follows his lead and layers in a second, slightly more revealing teaser before setting up the first PPV.

new_evidence.title: PPV Looping: Follow up within 5–10 seconds of any purchase to chain sales

variants.claim: The best time to sell a subscriber another PPV is immediately after they make any purchase, capitalizing on the 'f*** it' impulse to keep the buying momentum going.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters monitor their CRM (e.g. Infloww) for any purchase notification — old PPV, mass PPV, feed post unlock — and follow up within 5–10 seconds with a curiosity-hook message before the fan even finishes watching what they bought. The follow-up doesn't reference the old video; it teases the next thing ('I was literally just about to do something I don't usually do'). From that reply, the chatter plans to sell 5 sequential PPVs, each progressively more explicit or longer, so each one appears more valuable and justifies a higher price. This loop restarts every time the fan buys.

variants.claim: After the first PPV purchase, cycling between descriptive text exchange, free teasers, and the next PPV sell maximizes session revenue; stopping the moment the fan finishes or runs out of money protects the long-term relationship.
variants.what_they_do: Post-first-purchase loop: (1) ask 'what would you do now if you saw me like this?' to get descriptive text, (2) mirror his fantasy back, (3) insert free teaser (photo/voice/short clip — not enough to satisfy), (4) set up next PPV with yes-ladder, (5) sell. Repeat until fan orgasms or explicitly says no money. At that point, immediately pivot to relationship-building mode — pre-recorded 'had a great time' appreciation video, romantic post-session questions.

chatters know the second they get that notification on their inflow or whatever CRM you use, that they need to follow up with the subscriber instantly

Sell until you can sell, right? Until he's tapped out or he comes.

If a subscriber accepts a dropped price too easily, immediately add more PPVs to the bundle to recover revenue ('I had an idea — because it's you, I want to throw in some extra stuff').

new_evidence.topic: Minimum viable team structure for one creator

variants.claim: An agency running even one creator needs three non-chatter roles: an account manager (OF vault/feed), a social media manager (IG/Reddit/Twitter), and a model-facing manager who relays communication between backend and creator.
variants.what_they_do: Account manager owns the OF side; social media manager owns all platform posting and content direction; a 'model manager' (ideally first-world, trusted) sits between both and the creator — giving her content lists, flagging trending sounds, celebrating milestones, and fielding her daily questions. The owner checks in with managers (not every IC) once each morning; managers run the ICs (outreach VAs, editors, chatters). Beginners can start with 3-6 chatters + one chatter manager. once in the morning, you check in with all of the managers.

Communication between backend ops and creators runs through Josh/Evan directly, which doesn't scale. Palm's SMM consolidation (§11) is a tech fix; this is the human-layer equivalent that Palm lacks.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define and hire (or designate internally) the three-role stack as Palm scales past 3-4 creators: (1) OF account manager per cluster of creators, (2) SMM coordinator owning platform execution, (3) model-facing manager as creator relationship layer. new_evidence.topic: Creator quality bar — A-tier targeting vs. settling

variants.claim: Spending months outreaching for a single high-quality creator yields more revenue and less wasted effort than onboarding an easy-to-sign but unmotivated creator.

He targets creators with 5k–50k followers who already have or look suitable for OnlyFans. He argues a bad 3-month engagement versus a 3-month search for an A-tier creator is a false economy because agency leverage multiplies creator quality.

variants.what_they_do: Ellis explicitly rejects creators with low work ethic regardless of other factors. He qualifies inbound leads using story-driven case-study content (e.g. 'how we took a creator from 0 to 40K') that pre-filters serious creators, rather than vanity result screenshots. The content itself does the qualification work before any call.

new_evidence.topic: Tiered commission escalation to reduce signing friction
new_evidence.title: Start at 20-30% commission and escalate by milestone to earn the full split

variants.claim: New agencies without proven results should offer a tiered commission structure — low percentage at signing, escalating to 50% only after hitting defined revenue milestones — to reduce creator risk and incentivize agency performance.
variants.what_they_do: One speaker describes his first deal: 30% upfront, moving to 40% at $15k/month, then 50/50 above $30k. The logic is explicit: 'I didn't know anything about this game' and going in at 50% without proof is unjustifiable for the creator. The escalator also creates aligned incentives — the agency is motivated to grow the account because their own take increases. A complementary tactic: for established creators already making (e.g.) $5k/month, guarantee that floor no matter what the agency does in month one, demonstrating good faith.

variants.claim: Stating '50% revenue split' is not a real offer — agencies must articulate specific outcomes (more time, more money, viral growth) to differentiate, because every competitor leads with the same split.
variants.what_they_do: Agency frames its pitch around two core creator pain points: (1) inability to grow new fans and (2) lack of time to manage everything. The offer is sold as 'save your time, make more money' with concrete evidence (screenshots of revenue growth from existing clients). The 50% figure is justified by showing the creator went from $8K to $108K/month — the split is irrelevant when absolute earnings quadruple.

sources.quote: my first Creator I went in at 30% and I said at 30% I will start if we get past 15K it'll move to 40 and if I can get you past 30k we'll go 50/50 from there moving forward
what not to do is... my agency takes 50% blah blah. This is not a real offer. It is instead a revenue split. There is no value to that

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm sets commission % at onboarding as a fixed field (§7), but there is no documented tiered/milestone-based commission model or floor-guarantee tactic for established creators. No internal baseline exists for how Palm's splits are structured or justified. This is a §7 gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Develop a documented commission framework with two tracks: (1) New creator / unproven account — start at 30-35%, escalate to 50% upon hitting defined monthly revenue threshold (suggest $10k/month, consistent with the 3-month target the speakers reference). (2) Established creator already earning — offer a monthly floor guarantee equal to their current earnings; charge full split only on revenue above that floor in month one. Codify both in the onboarding contract wizard.

new_evidence.title: Multi-stage hiring funnel with hard filters before any offer

variants.claim: A slow, multi-stage hiring funnel with strict filtering criteria is cheaper than hiring the wrong person, whose true cost in lost time, training, and mistakes runs into tens of thousands of dollars.
variants.what_they_do: Agency runs a dedicated hiring funnel lasting roughly one week: job post → multiple filtering stages → skills/personality tests → pick only the single best-qualified applicant. If no applicant clears the bar, the owner does the job themselves for another 2 weeks and re-runs the funnel rather than settling. Once hired, the employee gets a fully thought-through onboarding, individual KPI tracking, and formal performance reviews.

variants.claim: Screen chatters on measurable competencies before hiring and training, since bad chatting silently destroys revenue.
variants.what_they_do: Before hiring a chatter, run an English skill test, a Wi-Fi speed test, and a words-per-minute test; only those who pass get hired and trained. Bad chatters (spelling/grammar errors, mispriced first PPVs like $4.99 or $200) are a top-7 agency-killing mistake.

we give them an English skill test... a Wi-Fi speed test... a words per minute test. Only once they check out on all of these, that is when you hire them.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's hiring is not formally documented (§10 — THIN). There is no written hiring funnel, scorecard, or onboarding SOP. palm_comparison.recommendation: Design a formal 2–3 stage hiring funnel for each role Palm needs next (e.g., editor, chatter, VA): job post → async task/test → live screen → offer. new_evidence.topic: AI reel animation — 'secret prompt' technique for virality
new_evidence.title: Extract the core action from a viral reel as the animation prompt to replicate its hook

variants.claim: The key to making an AI-animated reel go viral is identifying the specific physical action in the original viral reel and using that exact action as the animation prompt, rather than a generic description.
variants.what_they_do: When a viral reel is identified (e.g., 1M views), the operator distills the core action ('girl is doing a backflip') as the animation prompt fed into the AI tool (Kling). They then ask the AI tool to refine and make the prompt 'as realistic as possible.' The first frame of the recreated image is animated with this action prompt.

variants.claim: A fully AI-generated reel (no face swap, no real footage) avoids copyright and brand risk while still achieving viral reach when built from reverse-engineered prompts of high-performing inspo reels.
variants.what_they_do: Operator (1) identifies high-performing AI content on IG (targeting 100k–1M view reels, motion reels preferred), (2) screenshots the best frame, (3) feeds screenshot to Grok or ChatGPT to generate a detailed image prompt, (4) pastes prompt into Kling/MidJourney/CR to generate the image, (5) animates it in Kling, (6) adds captions in CapCut, (7) posts one reel/day for 10 days. At 10 posts they analyze which performed best and scale that template with a 'trial reel strategy' (20 reels/day).

sources.quote: the secret prompt is what the original reel is doing to make it viral… if the girl is doing a backflip the secret prompt is going to be the girl is doing a backflip
ask the deep fake to refine the prompt and make it as much realistic as possible

post one reel a day for 10 days… you're going to analyze the data for 10 days… see which one performs the best

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's §1 pipeline includes AI vision analysis of inspo reels (transcript, visual breakdown, hook, viral score) but the output is a filming idea for a real creator, not an animation prompt for synthetic video. This technique is specifically for AI content production and has no current Palm equivalent. The underlying logic — isolate the hook action, not just the aesthetic — is relevant to Palm's brief-writing for real creators too.
palm_comparison.recommendation: For the AI persona build-toward (§3/§0): incorporate 'action extraction prompt' as a named step in the synthetic reel SOP — after screenshotting inspo, explicitly ask GPT 'what is the specific physical action happening in this reel?' and use that answer as the primary animation prompt. new_evidence.topic: Reel posting cadence — structured test window
new_evidence.title: 10-day / 1-reel-per-day structured test before scaling posting frequency

new_evidence.title: Set explicit month-1/2/3 milestones with creator at onboarding
